column4: describing 614 botanical species belonging to 358 genera and 89 families

WOOD CHEMICAL COMPOSITION DATABASE

The CIRAD wood chemical composition database is a part of the general CIRAD wood database covering a wide range of technological properties
column7: mainly physics and mechanics
column8: anatomy
column9: natural durability and preservation
column10: wood machining
column11:
etc.
614 wood species are described with measurements of wood polymers (cellulose
column12: lignin and pentosan) and overall extraneous components (ethanol-benzene
column14: ash
column15:
silica content). These measurements were taken between 1945 and 1990 using the same standard protocols.
Chemical characteristics were measured on specimen from 1
column17:
358 genera and 89 families.

DESCRIPTION OF THE DATASET

It consists in the following file: Cirad chemical wood composition database - dataset‧xlsx

General information

The file is in XLSX format.

Data fields

The file provides the following characteristics for each specimen (also given in the second tab of the file Cirad chemical wood composition - dataset‧xlsx):

• Test: Each individual set of measurements
• Year: Calendar year of the measurement
• Tree: CTFT reference in the wood collection
• Origin: Provenance country of the tree
• Family: Botanical family (updated version June 2018)
• Species: Botanical genus and species (updated version June 2018)
• Density Code: 1- value from Cirad wood collection; 2- value from literature; 3- missing value
• Density: Value from wood collection or sometimes from literature
• AB ext: Ethanol/benzene extract (%)
• W ext: Water extract (%)
• Ash: Mineral content (%)
• Silica: Silica content (%)
• Lignin: Lignin content (%)
• Pentosan: Pentosan content (%)
• Cellulose: Cellulose content (%)
• Balance: Summation of all contents except silica (%)

% values are always related to anhydrous wood gross weight

column4: Andoniaina Valérie‡"This reference database in vector format (ESRI shape format) is organized according to a multi-level nomenclature. It is used to train an image classification algorithm in order to produce land cover maps of the Antananarivo metropolitan area (see dataverse sheets describing these maps). In order to simplify the work we chose to update the database produced in 2017 and described in: Dupuy et all
column5: 2020 (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dib.2020.105952). All polygons were verified by photo-interpretation of the Pleiades image acquired on April 3
column6:
2022. GPS points were collected to update some classes and take into account changes since 2017. Each GPS point was converted into a polygon by digitizing the boundaries of the corresponding parcel on a Pleiades image (pixel size of 0.5 m * 0.5 m). These polygons cover the entire study area in order to have a representative view of the existing crop types and urban structures. The final database contains 3113 polygons.

column8:
2022 we publish a new version to limit the effects related to flooding (the wetland class was overestimated on the previous version of land cover map).

The cassava breeding product profile for Gari-Eba has to to align with the traits demanded by producers as well as processors and consumers especially because the Gari-Eba food product includes so many labour and energy demanding processing stages. RTB foods work package 1 research activities therefore included literature review
column23: surveys with all value chain actors
column24: participatory processing and consumer testing to illicit key traits important for especially processors and consumers in high cassava producing and consuming states in the Southwest (Osun) and North Central (Benue). A triangulation of all the data obtained prioritized key quality characteristics/traits (PQT). At pre-harvest these are early bulking
column25: weed competitiveness
column27: and stem storability; at harvest (raw root stage) these are high yield with good root size/shape
column28: high dry matter and multiple use of cassava. High dry matter and its stability cut across all the evaluations stages. During processing
column29: PQTs include minimal processing drudgery (including ease of peeling)
column30: non-discolouration and product yield. For the Gari intermediate and Eba final product
column31: colour and amount of swelling are both important while texture is crucial to Eba. Gender impact scores per trait validate that the top PQTs are all essential-must have traits from a gender perspective across the various stages. For instance
column32: the multiple product-use of cassava enables men and especially women to make choices related to profit
column33: household food needs
column34: and different workload involved for different final products. There might be trade-offs in simultaneously stressing traits majorly important for women. Triangulated results complement earlier evidence (Teeken et al.
column36: https://doi.org/10.1111/ijfs.14862) indicating that in cultures that use more fermentation there could be a trade-off between high yield and dry matter on the one hand and food product quality on the other. Concerning processing drudgery
column37: there seems to be a trade-off with regards to ease of peeling. High dry matter roots are often less easy to peel and this less easiness to peel is often considered by processors as an indication that the root has less water
column38: so is positively appreciated. Further inquiry is needed into the relation between drudgery

column3: Gilles‡Increasing requirements for developing tools allowing fine traceability of strains responsible for epidemics is tightly linked with the need to understand factors shaping pathogen populations and their interactions with their environment. Bacterial wilt (BW) caused by the Ralstonia solanacearum species complex (RSSC) causes one of the most important plant diseases in tropical and sub-tropical regions. Sadly few
column4: outdated or no information on its epidemiology is reported in the literature
column5:
although alarming outbreaks are regularly reported as a disaster both in terms of economical and sociological impact.

column6: 608) belonging to the Ralstonia solanacearum species complex Phylotype I strains was retrieved from diseased plants on the field across the South West Indian Ocean and Africa. This collection helped further assessing the epidemiological discriminating power of the RS1-MLVA14 scheme previously published. A total of 13 markers were validated and characterized as not equally informative
column7: with little infra-sequevar polymorphism for most of them and performance-specific depending on the Phylotype I lineage considered. column3: Lionel‡Dans le cadre du Centre d’Expertise Scientifique Occupation des Sols de THEIA
column4: l’UMR TETIS du CIRAD développe une méthode de cartographie d’occupation du sol automatisée intitulée Moringa . La méthodologie consiste en une segmentation d’une image à Très Haute Résolution Spatiale (identification d'objets homogènes sur l'image THRS) puis une classification de chaque objet homogène par l’extraction d’indices d’une ou de plusieurs séries temporelles d’images à haute résolution spatiale et temporelle ; la classification est entrainée sur une base de données d’apprentissage réalisée par photo-interprétation et expertise de terrain. La collaboration entre l’UMR Tétis et l’UR AïDA a permis la production de la carte d’occupation du sol pour l'année 2022. Elle utilise une mosaïque d'images Spot6/7 (acquisitions Mai-Juin 2022) pour la segmentation puis toutes les images Sentinel2 de cette même année pour la classification. La base de données de terrain est constituée de plus de 5000 objets actualisés en 2022 et caractérisés selon 3 niveaux d’une nomenclature emboîtée comportant de 4 à 29 classes. Le niveau le plus détaillé distinguant les types de cultures présente une précision globale de 90% et un indice de Kappa est de 0
column5: 89. Le niveau 2 (12 classes)
column6: distinguant les groupes de cultures présente une précision globale de 92% et un indice de Kappa est de 0
column7: 91. Le niveau 1 (4 classes)
column8: distinguant les grands groupes d'occupation du sol présente une précision globale de 98% et un indice de Kappa est de 0

[FR] Données sur les ratios isotopiques (C et N) et sur la contamination de la macrofaune du sol par la chlordécone (CLD) et un de ses produits de transformation : le chlordécole (CLDOH). Les invertébrés du sol ont été collectés sur les parcelles du projet BANABIO situées sur la station expérimentale de Rivière Lézarde. Le delta 15 et le Delta 13C ainsi que les niveaux de contamination en CLD et CLDOH ont été mesurés sur 14 espèces d'invertébrés et un lézard prédateurs représentatifs des réseaux trophiques du sol des agroécosytèmes de Martinique. Pour chaque espèce
column4: plusieurs individus ont été regroupés afin d'atteindre une masse suffisante pour permettre l'analyse des 2 molécules étudiées. Les analyses de contamination ont été réalisées par l'équipe Pesticides et biotoxines marines (PBM) de l'Agence nationale de sécurité sanitaire de l'alimentation
column5: de l'environnement et du travail (ANSES) à Maison-Alfort (France). Les analyses isotopiques ont été analysées à l'Atelier de Quantifications Isotopiques (AQUI) de l'unité de recherche B&PMP à Montpellier

column3: Luis‡We proposed an approach to solve the inverse problem for tree imaging
column4: using a method based on ultrasound travel-time tomography and adapted to the anisotropy of wood material. The proposed inversion procedure was developed between 2015-2018. The method considers the dependency of the wave velocity on the angle of propagation and the wood elastic constants
column5: as described by the Christoffel equation. This dependency leads to curved trajectories (or rays)
column6: that are followed by the wave from the source to the receivers
column7: affecting the time-of-flight (TOF) measurements. A first inversion is performed considering an hypothesis of straight-line rays
column8: using an algebraic reconstruction algorithm (SIRT)
column9: adapting the matrix formulation to obtain the slowness for every pixel as a polynomial approximation of the Christoffel equation. Then
column10: the obtained set of slowness functions are used to perform the direct (or forward) problem
column11: simulating the wave propagation using a raytracing approach. This simulation allows to estimate the curved trajectories and synthetic TOF values
column12: that are then compared to the experimental measurements. This process is repeated iteratively until convergence. From the final iteration
column13: the polynomial approximations of the Christoffel equations for each pixel are used to obtain the elastic constants via a non-linear regression. Finally
column14: the parametric image is presented. The data folder contains the time-of-flight measurements (in seconds) and the sensors positions (in meters) from an experimental setting using pine samples. A photography of the cross-section is included. TOF values are organized as a matrix (sinogram)
column15: where columns correspond to each source
column16: and receivers to each row. Sensors positions are presented as a column vector
column17: specifying the [X
column18: Y] coordinates. Three different configurations were tested: (1) a healthy case
column19: (2) a centered defect case and (3) an off-centered defect case. Defects were simulated by drilling a circular hole of diameter 7.6 cm. The forward folder contains the code to perform the raytracing simulation (direct problem)
column20: used in the iterative inversion schema. The main file is rayPix‧m
column21: that performs the raytracing. The inverse folder contains the code to perform the image reconstruction (inverse problem). The main file is optimFinal‧m
column22: where the iterative schema was implemented.  To perform an inversion
column23: the directives are in mainInv‧m‡CIRAD; Wood; Tomography; Ultrasound; Forestry - General aspects‡Dataset; Software‡"1_PhotoHealthy.png; 1_posRecHealthy.txt; 1_TOFHealthy.txt; 2_PhotoDefCen.png; 2_posRecDefCen.txt; 2_TOFDefCen.txt; 3_PhotoDefEcc.png; 3_posRecDefEcc.txt; 3_TOFDefEcc.txt; approxPaths.txt; calcAngle.txt; calcDispla.txt; calcNormals.txt; calcRaysR.txt; compAngDis.txt; critereAlg.txt; deleteCross.txt; filtrer.txt; funcInv.txt; interpInv.txt; inverseP.txt; inverseSIRT.txt; mainInv.txt; nonlinfit.txt; optimFinal.txt; plotTraj.txt; poly3find.txt; propWavePix.txt; rayPix.txt; README.txt; rotMatrix.txt; smoothCurvePix.txt"‡‡‡CIRAD; Wood; Tomography; Ultrasound; Forestry - General aspects‡CIRAD; Wood; Tomography; Ultrasound; Forestry - General aspects‡English‡‡‡‡‡

column3: Ibadan
column4: Nigeria‡Adesokan Michael‡Adesokan
column5: Michael‡The near-infrared hyperspectral images of freshly prepared Gari_Eba produced from 23 cassava genotypes were acquired using the SPECIM FX 17 Camera. Gari_Eba dough was homogenized and placed on the translation stage for a line scan image capture. The hyperspectral image was captured in the 900 to 1700 nm wavelength range and has a spectral resolution of 8 nm. Reflectance calibration and image segmentation were carried out using the Breeze Software (version 2024.0.1) to extract the average spectra from each image. An attempt to predict the textural attributes of Gari_Eba was performed by combining the samples’ average spectra with the reference texture values. NIR hyperspectral imaging demonstrated a promising prediction performance for the hardness of Eba but could not accurately predict other textural attributes of Eba. Further attempts will be made by extracting the effective wavelengths from the average raw spectra to improve the model's accuracy by removing the redundant information. The coefficient of determination in calibration for hardness was 0.90 and prediction performance of 0.76 respectively.‡CIRAD; hyperspectral imaging; infrared spectrophotometry; spectral analysis; garri; eba; image segmentation; image analysis; PLSR; Q - Food science and technology‡Dataset‡"RTB Breeding Quality_HTP Dataset_HSI_Eba_IITA_Nigeria_16072024.tab"‡CC BY-NC-SA 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0‡CIRAD; hyperspectral imaging; infrared spectrophotometry; spectral analysis; garri; eba; image segmentation; image analysis; PLSR; Q - Food science and technology‡CIRAD; hyperspectral imaging; infrared spectrophotometry; spectral analysis; garri; eba; image segmentation; image analysis; PLSR; Q - Food science and technology‡English‡‡‡‡‡

column3: Mathieu‡"[FR]Données de croissance et de rendement agronomique de bananiers cavendish cultivés en Martinique dans le cadre du projet BANABIO visant à l' « Evaluation de systèmes de culture innovants de BANane BIOlogique ». Comparaison de 2 itinéraires techniques en agriculture biologique : (1) Bio-intensif (parcelles en monoculture avec une densité de bananiers de 1800 plans/ha conduites en AB) et (2) Bio-diversifié (parcelles avec association de bananiers et de cacaoyers dans un système agroforestier avec une densité de bananiers de 1200 plans/ha) par rapport à un itinéraire technique (3) conventionnel (parcelles en monoculture avec une densité de 1800 plans/ha conduites selon la moyenne des pratiques en vigueur en Martinique au moment de l'essai). column4: Simon‡"This dataset consists of land cover maps for the district of Murewa in Zimbabwe
column5: for the year 2023.   The dataset was created as part of the RAIZ (Resilience building through agroecological intensification in Zimbabwe) project FOOD/2021/424- 933 (https://raiz.org.zw/)
column6: founded by the European Union.  The land cover maps were generated using PlanetScope mosaics provided by the NICFI Program. Monthly mosaics
column7: covering the period from June 2022 to June 2023
column8: are provided at a 5m spatial resolution.  A deep learning model based on a 3D convolutional neural network (CNN) was used to process the satellite images. The images of the different dates are concatenated
column9: and patches of 17x17 pixels are used as input
column10: with the model predicting the class of the central pixel. This approach enables the model to learn spatial patterns in the images as well as temporal information. The inputs to the model included all available bands (blue
column11: green
column13: and near infrared) as well as the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI).    Land Use Classes:     • Dense Woodlands (0): Areas with a dense canopy where grass is not visible from above. Trees are typically over 2 meters tall.     • Cropland (1): Land used for agricultural purposes
column14: including fields and farms for crop cultivation.     • Grassland (2): Areas dominated by grass
column15: with occasional shrubs and trees covering less than 20% of the area. These are primarily open grasslands.     • Open Woodlands (3): Savanna woodlands with a sparse canopy of trees (over 2 meters tall) and scattered shrubs.     • Mineral Soils (4): Rocky areas
column17: locally referred to as ""Dwalas.""     • Built-up Surface (5): Urbanized areas that include buildings
column18: roads
column19: and other man-made structures.     • Bare soil (6): Areas characterized by exposed earth with minimal to no vegetation      • Grassland Vlei (7): Wet grassland areas
column20: typically found in low-lying or floodplain regions
column21: where water accumulates seasonally.     • Water (8): Bodies of water such as rivers
column23: and ponds."‡CIRAD; land use; land cover; landscape; remote sensing; landsat; U30 - Méthodes et techniques de recherche‡Collection; Dataset; Image‡"Confusion_Matrix.png; Illustration.png; land_use_map.tif; Per-class-metrics.png"‡CC BY-NC 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0‡CIRAD; land use; land cover; landscape; remote sensing; landsat; U30 - Méthodes et techniques de recherche‡CIRAD; land use; land cover; landscape; remote sensing; landsat; U30 - Méthodes et techniques de recherche‡‡‡‡‡‡

Ce(tte) œuvre est mise à disposition selon les termes de la Licence Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International."‡Dioscorea alata orthologous groups identified by comparative genomics approach‡Chaïr Hana‡Zotta Mota Ana‡Yams are important tubers widely consumed in developing countries in various forms
column3: pounded and fried. Tuber quality is a key criterion for acceptance by the various actors in the value chain. However
column4: the genetics of yam tuber quality has not yet been comprehensively investigated. Given this lack of knowledge
column5: we used population genomics and candidate gene association approaches to unravel the genetic basis of the three main quality attributes of boiled yam
column6: namely texture
column7: starch content and colour indices. We re-sequenced the whole genome of 127 yam genotypes with different quality attributes
column8: and performed an enrichment of the already available functional genome annotation using comparative genomics. Population genomics analysis highlighted three main genetic groups and 1
column9: 053 genes under selection. We focused this work on three known quality trait-related pathways: pectin
column10: starch content and flavonoid biosynthesis
column11: and inventoried in the genome all the structural genes related to those pathways by comparative genomics. We improved the functional annotation of the three pathways with 48% more genes. A total of 18 candidate genes containing genetic variants significantly associated with the target traits were detected
column12: including eight genes that were also found under selection. The texture-related candidate genes were distributed between the pectin and starch pathways. Overall
column13: the use of comparative genomics has allowed the generation of an unprecedented genomic resource. The improved functional annotation of the yam genome is a promising predictive tool for identifying other core genes associated with any trait of interest to meet the growing need and diversified demands for yams. To complement the set of Dioscorea alata annotated genes
column14: a comparative genomics approach was employed using a comparison of 45 genomes of species from diverse plant lineages. The species were chosen based on their genomes
column15: phylogenetic distribution along the tree of viridiplantae and in the use of plant-models. They belong to the monocotyledons and dicotyledons
column16: encompassing 17 different clades. The orthology analysis resulted in a total of 58
column17: 916 ortholous groups (OG) from 1
column19: 645 proteins. The OG were first used in this study to complement the set of genes in pathways involved in yam tuber quality.‡CIRAD; Comparative genomics; Dioscorea; Plant genetics and breeding; Food science and technology - General aspects‡Dataset‡"Orthogroups.GeneCount.tab; Orthogroups.txt; Statistics_Overall.txt; Statistics_PerSpecies.txt"‡‡‡CIRAD; Comparative genomics; Dioscorea; Plant genetics and breeding; Food science and technology - General aspects‡CIRAD; Comparative genomics; Dioscorea; Plant genetics and breeding; Food science and technology - General aspects‡English‡‡‡‡‡

column5: Herman‡Les interfaces entre les aires protégées et leurs périphéries en Afrique australe sont sujettes à des interactions entre la faune et le bétail qui varient en fréquence et en intensité. Dans ces zones
column6: la juxtaposition entre les utilisations des terres de production et de conservation dans un contexte d'enthronisation croissante peut créer des problèmes liés à la coexistence entre l'homme et la faune et soulève des préoccupations pour la conservation de la biodiversité
column7: le développement local et les moyens de subsistance.  Les interfaces sauvage/domestique jouent un rôle prépondérant dans l'émergence d'agents pathogènes (Despommier et al.
column8: 2006) en raison d'interactions importantes entre l'homme
column9: les espèces domestiquées et la faune sauvage dans l'espace et dans le temps (Haydon et al.
column10: 2002). Ces transmissions d'agents pathogènes sont naturellement bidirectionnelles entre le réservoir et les populations sensibles (sauvage -> domestique vs domestique -> sauvage) et peuvent donc être potentiellement spatialisées autour de « points chauds » d'interactions espèces sauvages/domestiques. La proximité spatiale entre les populations a été clairement identifiée comme l'un des facteurs de risque cruciaux dans la transmission entre espèces (Roche et al.
column11: 2012).  L'objectif principal est de modéliser à partir des données d'observation de la Terre

column4: Traore‡Choosing plantain varieties adapted to local conditions in these times of climate change requires knowledge of their agronomic performance and disease resistance. The aim of this study was to obtain agronomic data from plantain genotypes grown under local conditions for the production of aloco (fried plantain). A total of 12 plantain genotypes were grown in June 2023. During their growth
column5: measurements were taken on the number of leaves at flowering and harvest
column6: plant size
column7: cercosporiasis infection index
column8: etc. At harvest
column9: buches mass
column10: number of hands and number of fingers were determined. The results showed significant variations between genotypes
column11: both in terms of disease susceptibility and physical development. Some genotypes
column13: showed superior performance in terms of growth and pseudo-trunk circumference
column14: while others
column16: showed less favorable results. The infection index revealed that varieties such as SACI
column17: Orishele
column18: and Big Ebanga were the most susceptible to cercosporiosis disease
column19: while Corne bout rond and Zakoi proved more resistant. In terms of agromorphology
column20: notable differences were observed in the number of leaves
column21: with Fiha 21 recording the highest number at flowering and harvest
column22: while varieties such as Orishele showed the lowest number of leaves. In addition
column23: the height of the banana plants varied significantly

column3: Babacar‡The tree inventory was conducted in the Cordyla pinnata parkland in Senegal
column4: in the region of Nioro du Rip. A field campaign for tree species data collection was carried out in February 2019. It is a weighted stratified sampling of 213 observation sites distributed according to a landscape heterogeneity gradient. At each site

column6: extra species (not present in the plot) observed within a radius of 400 m were included to increase the odds of recording all species in the area. Individual tree location and species were recorded with a Garmin GSMAP 64 GPS device. Due to a GPS-reported accuracy of 3 m

column3: Jacques‡The wood collection (Xylotheque or Xylarium) of Kourou (French Guiana) was created in 1976 by Jean Thiel and has since been augmented by many contributors. It is a child collection of the Xylotheque of Montpellier. A Xylarium
column4: like the one in Kourou
column5: is a unique collection of wood assembled over many years that represents high interspecific diversity. In addition to the standard use of such collections for systematic anatomy
column6: a technological database can be created using duplicates of reference plates. We performed physical
column7: mechanical and durability tests on wood planks measuring 10x60x100L mm3 from 445 woody species in French Guiana belonging to 63 families. Wood densities ranged from 250 to 1300 kg/m3
column8: with an average of 0.786 kg/dm3. The other properties also covered a wide range of values
column9: as did the longitudinal modulus of elasticity
column10:
since we tested samples from lianas and palm stipes as well as from very stiff woods.

column3: Agneta‡"The Consortium Europe-Africa on Research and Innovation for Food Systems Transformation (CEA-FIRST) is a bicontinental effort put together to operationalise the International Research Consortium (IRC) as a long-term platform on Food and Nutrition Security and Sustainable Agriculture (FNSSA). One of the objectives of Work Package 2 of this project is to consolidate the existing FNSSA knowledge management system (KMS) tools to facilitate public access and knowledge sharing on relevant research activities and results. In this context
column4: we have built the IRC lexicon to be integrated into the IRC knowledge management platform to fulfil semantic analysis and indexing needs. IRC lexicon aims to encompass the relevant themes in relation to the FNSSA roadmap. The lexicon is the combination of 4 existing semantic resources
column5: i.e. inputs to construct the final lexicon:  <ol>  <li> The FNSSA database keywords

corresponding to the keywords used by SLU librarians to tag the documents’ topics
  • The LEAP4FNSSA lexicon

built during the first stage of the project (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S235234092200885X)

food security)

column3: Gérard‡Après les cyclones Iris
column4: Luis et Marylin de la fin d'année 1995 qui faisaient suite à la tempête Debby de 1994
column5:
de profondes modifications sont intervenues dans le paysage bananier. Ce projet avait pour objectif de pouvoir disposer pour la première fois en Guadeloupe d'une cartographie à grande échelle ( 1/5 000eme ) du parcellaire bananier et par la suite d'une estimation statistique très précise des superficies.

Les travaux ont été menés à partir d'une mission aérienne spécifique au 1/20 000 réalisée en 1996 et ont permis de mesurer et positionner à 1m près toutes les parcelles de taille supérieure ou égale à un quart d'hectare puis d'estimer les superficies avec une erreur globale maximale de 3% soit 150 ha.

Une enquête terrain complémentaire a été effectuée en 1997 en collaboration avec les SICA
column6:
les Groupements de Producteurs et la Chambre d'Agriculture de Guadeloupe.

Cette grande précision à la fois cartographique et statistique a pu être obtenue par l'utilisation des techniques de la photogrammétrie pour restituer les surfaces projetées
column7: les points cotés et les courbes de niveau
column8:
mais aussi grâce à la création d'un Modèle Numérique de Terrain (MNT) qui a permis de caractériser chaque parcelle par sa pente et de calculer les surfaces développées réellement cultivées (le quart des surfaces est cultivé sur des pentes supérieures à 30%).

column3: Adrien‡Weed pressure is a main biotic constraint in tropical agriculture. Cover crop mixtures have increased in popularity to limit weed growth through competition for shared resources but the relation between cover crop diversity and weed suppression is still under debate. This study aimed to assess the impact of increasing cover crops diversity (one to four species) on weed control during two 4-month growing seasons (tropical summer and winter) in Reunion Island. l’application qui s’exécute sur une tablette Android permet de :
  • enregistrer de nouvelles mesures sur des arbres recrutés lors des campagnes antérieures ;
  • ajouter et mesurer de nouveaux recrutés ;
  • visualiser une liste des arbres non encore mesurés ;
  • exporter un fichier des nouvelles mesures pour permettre de le récupérer et le transférer.
L'application utilise un langage de programmation connu et proche du Web (Javascript) avec la possibilité d'utiliser les composants natifs du système d’exploitation (Android). column2: cooperatives and livelihoods:: survey in six Indian villages‡Gaillard Cedric‡Gaillard Cedric‡India's dairy sector has emerged as the world's largest dairy producer and has enabled 70 million farmers to generate income through its rapid growth. This success is linked to broad national policy support through the Operation Flood program and the emergence of an inclusive model of cooperatives. However
column3: the informal sector is still the marketing channel most used by dairy producers
column4: and with the liberalization of the dairy sector
column5: the cooperative model is also facing competition from the private sector. By surveying 244 dairy farmers in two major but heterogeneous states in India
column6: this paper examines the inclusiveness of the sector and the impact of dairy cooperative membership on farmers’ income and livelihood. The originality of the paper concerns its systematic perspective on households’ assets and activities. The results indicate that cooperative membership is associated with caste membership and with proximity to cooperative collection centers. Better incomes are associated with membership
column7: particularly among farmers with less land and among smallholders
column8: who are more dependent on their dairy income to lift themselves out of poverty.‡CIRAD; Dairy Farming; Livelihoods; Dairy cooperavatives; Ex : Seed production and processing‡Dataset‡‡CC BY-NC 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0‡CIRAD; Dairy Farming; Livelihoods; Dairy cooperavatives; Ex : Seed production and processing‡CIRAD; Dairy Farming; Livelihoods; Dairy cooperavatives; Ex : Seed production and processing‡‡‡‡‡‡

column4: focusing on their interactions with psyllids as a primary food resource. The research investigates the effects of landscape composition

column6:
and prey availability on the structure of all ladybird species associated with psyllids and specifically on the presence and abundance of species whose life cycles depend on psyllids.

column7: focusing on two plant species heavily infested with psyllids: Leucaena leucocephala and Acacia heterophylla. Ladybirds and psyllids were regularly collected over a two-year period using a thermal aspirator
column8: complemented by visual inspection. column3: dans le district de Mananara (Madagascar) en 2017‡Penot
column4: Eric‡De Rouvroy
column5:
Charline‡L’étude présentée ici fait partie du projet FORECAST. L’objectif de ce projet est de récolter des données pour les outils d’analyse et de spatialisation (Olympe et MapVillage) pour ainsi appréhender les systèmes agricoles en milieu forestier et comprendre les stratégies paysannes.
Cette étude s’est déroulée dans une région enclavée de Madagascar : Mananara-Nord et a pour objectif de comprendre l’importance des différentes cultures
column6: notamment celle du giroflier et de la vanille
column7: de l’élevage et des activités off-farm
column8:
dans la constitution des revenus des ménages agricoles et dans le système agroforestier. Elle a aussi pour objectif de connaître la résilience des exploitations face aux changements économiques et climatiques du point de vue de la stabilité des revenus et de la sécurité alimentaire.
L’analyse des données récoltées sur trois sites se base une démarche quantitative des performances technico-économiques à l’échelle du système agroforestiers et du système d’activité sur 2015 et 2016. Cette période étant caractérisée par la flambée du cours de la vanille
column9:
cette culture fait figure de première source de revenu pour plus de la moitié des ménages enquêtés. Le giroflier a tout de même son importance pour les ménages agricoles du district de Mananara-Nord.
En ce qui concerne la modélisation sur Olympe
column10: deux scénarios ont été simulés. Le scénario du « retour des prix de la vanille à la normale » montre que les types dont la première source de revenu est la vanille sont les plus impactés économiquement. Toutefois
column11: en terme de résilience
column12: les exploitations qui donnent priorité a la vanille restent toujours plus résiliente que celles qui priorisent le clou de girofle
column13: grâce à leur diversification. Le scenario du « passage du cyclone » montre que tous les types d’exploitations subissent une baisse durable après le passage d’un cyclone dévastateur de type Gafilo. Cette baisse est surtout marquée sur les exploitations dont la première source de revenu est le girofle‡CIRAD; Syzygium aromaticum; agroforesterie; agroforestry; Madagascar; modélisation; modelling; revenu; income; systèmes agroforestiers; agroforestry systems; clou de girofle; cloves; Mananara; Economie de la production; Production economics; Méthodes mathématiques et statistiques; Mathematical and statistical methods‡Dataset‡"Mananara_2017 anonym.OLY"‡CC BY-NC 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0‡CIRAD; Syzygium aromaticum; agroforesterie; agroforestry; Madagascar; modélisation; modelling; revenu; income; systèmes agroforestiers; agroforestry systems; clou de girofle; cloves; Mananara; Economie de la production; Production economics; Méthodes mathématiques et statistiques; Mathematical and statistical methods‡CIRAD; Syzygium aromaticum; agroforesterie; agroforestry; Madagascar; modélisation; modelling; revenu; income; systèmes agroforestiers; agroforestry systems; clou de girofle; cloves; Mananara; Economie de la production; Production economics; Méthodes mathématiques et statistiques; Mathematical and statistical methods‡‡‡‡‡‡

column3:
Mathieu‡CONTEXTE : projet CRESI BigDataPol (http://textmining.biz/Projects/BigDataPol). But : Mobiliser des approches de Big data pour l'analyse des processus et des effets des politiques publiques dans le milieu rural.
Question de recherche en SHS adaptée au terrain Guadeloupe : (1) Participation citoyenne dans le processus de construction des politiques agri-environnementales. (2) Opinions de différents acteurs sur les processus d’élaboration et les effets des politiques publiques relatives à l’agroécologie en Guadeloupe.
Choix d’un objet de recherche (terrain Guadeloupe) dans le cadre du Projet CRESI BigDataPol : Exemple des controverses au sujet des traitements aériens contre la cercosporiose des bananiers (car contestation citoyenne et succession d’interdiction/dérogation fruit d’un rapport de force entre société civile et producteurs de banane) en Guadeloupe.
TERMES EXTRAITS :
Termes automatiquement identifiés avec BioTex avec une extraction et un classement selon 4 stratégies sur 2 corpus : (1) all : termes simples et composés
column4: (2) multi : termes composés
column5: (3) C-value : classement qui peut privilégier les termes les plus longs
column6:
(4) F_TFIDF_C : classement qui prend en compte une notion de discriminance.
Le données contiennent également une sélection des 100 premiers termes retournés par chaque mesure/stratégie associés à chaque corpus (fichiers termes_corpus1_BigDataPol_09122017.txt et termes_corpus2_BigDataPol_09122017.txt)
Les deux corpus utilisés pour effectuer cette extraction de la terminologie sont : (1) corpus 1 : Corpus de textes issus de la « Société civile » (associations de protection de l’environnement et LKP)
column7: (2) Corpus 2 : corpus de textes issus du « Groupement des producteurs de banane de Guadeloupe ».‡CIRAD; analyse des politiques publiques; fouille de données; data mining; E14 - Économie et politique du développement; E50 - Sociologie rurale‡Text; Dataset‡"termes_all_cvalue_corpus1V7.xml; termes_all_cvalue_corpus2V4.xml; termes_all_F-TFIDF_C_corpus1V7.xml; termes_all_F-TFIDF_C_corpus2V4.xml; termes_corpus1_BigDataPol_09122017.txt; termes_corpus2_BigDataPol_09122017.txt; termes_multi_cvalue_corpus1V7.xml; termes_multi_cvalue_corpus2V4.xml; termes_multi_F-TFIDF_C_corpus1V7.xml; termes_multi_F-TFIDF_C_corpus2V4.xml"‡CC BY-NC-SA 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0‡CIRAD; analyse des politiques publiques; fouille de données; data mining; E14 - Économie et politique du développement; E50 - Sociologie rurale‡CIRAD; analyse des politiques publiques; fouille de données; data mining; E14 - Économie et politique du développement; E50 - Sociologie rurale‡French‡‡‡‡‡

column3: Stéphane‡The land cover maps published here were produced for four regions of the northern part of Chin state in Myanmar: Hakha
column4: Falam
column5: Tedim and Thantlang. This work was carried out as part of the ALIVE FNS project to monitor land use (forests
column6: cultivated land
column7: built-up areas
column8: etc.). We used the Moringa processing chain
column9: which is based on satellite imagery (Sentinel 2 free of charge time series and SPOT6-7 very high spatial resolution) and a supervised classification algorithm (Random Forest) trained on a reference database made of polygons associated with a land cover class. Generally
column10: this database comes from ground GPS surveys
column11: but it can be replaced by photo-interpretation of very high spatial resolution images if field collection is unavailable or impossible
column12:
as it is the case here in the State of Chin. The database was therefore obtained by photo-interpretation of Spot6/7 images acquired as part of the Dinamis programme.
The nomenclature includes 4 crop classes (irrigated crops - mainly rice
column14: new shifting cultivation
column15: old shifting cultivation) and 6 non-crop classes (open spaces with little or no vegetation
column17: shrubland
column18: wooded vegetation
column19: water
column20: built-up areas). The maps are available
column22: at a spatial resolution of 1.5 m over the parts covered by SPOT6/7 imagery (approximately half of the study area) and at a spatial resolution of 10m using only Sentinel-2 imagery  over the whole area comprising the 4 regions: Hakha
column25:
Thantlang.

column3: Ernest Ogbete‡Fufu
column4:
a fermented wet paste food product from cassava is a good source of dietary energy and is one of the widely consumed food in Nigeria and other West African countries. A rapid and accurate method for determining dry matter (DM) content of fufu mash will be valuable for further work to be done on fufu.

This data base contain the spectra of wet fufu mash samples processed from cassava genotypes harvested at NRCRI Umudike Nigeria (between August and September 2020) with the Portable/handheld NIRS (Quality SpecTrek). A total number of 540 spectra were acquired from 60 (sixty) samples of wet fufu mashes. Three different quartz cups were filled with fufu mashes (of about 8-10g) and covered with a white cover material. They were placed against the window of the portable/handheld NIRS device and spectra captured. Calibration model developed for dry matter content (DM) showed the mean and standard deviation to be 45.22 and 6.9 respectively with the standard error of laboratory at 0.79.

A SOP on the protocol for the determination of DM on wet fufu mash using a handheld NIRS has been linked to this database. The ASD hand-held NIRS was acquired by NRCRI (National Root Crops Research Institute) Umudike Nigeria in the year 2016.‡CIRAD; fufu; cassava; quality traits; quality; dry matter; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; standard error of laboratory (SEL); food analysis; Food science and Technology‡Dataset‡"RTBfoods_K.2.31_Spectral database_NIRS_Dry Matter_Wet Fufu_NRCRI Nigeria_2020.tab"‡‡‡CIRAD; fufu; cassava; quality traits; quality; dry matter; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; standard error of laboratory (SEL); food analysis; Food science and Technology‡CIRAD; fufu; cassava; quality traits; quality; dry matter; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; standard error of laboratory (SEL); food analysis; Food science and Technology‡English‡‡‡‡‡

column1: https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/ENA9LO‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/ENA9LO‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/ENA9LO‡file://10.18167/DVN1/ENA9LO‡‡doi:10.18167/DVN1/ENA9LO‡2025-02-12T05:31:01Z‡2024-07-09T15:24:39Z‡‡Replication Data for: Experimental evaluation of zinc and copper terrestrial ecotoxicity prediction by life cycle assessment in agricultural recycling of livestock effluent‡BRAVIN
column3: Emma‡The agronomic benefits of organic waste application on farmland can be overshadowed by the potential health and environmental impacts associated with trace element emissions
column4: especially copper (Cu) and zinc (Zn). This has prompted a need for life cycle assessment (LCA) models to predict their terrestrial comparative toxicity potential (CTP) and impact score. We compared the LCA (reference method) prediction ability with that obtained with an experimental dataset obtained from a month-long incubation experiment (experimental method) on a soil amended or not with 31 livestock effluents mimicking agronomically-relevant scenarios. The impact scores for Cu and Zn differed between the reference and experimental methods due to CTP discrepancies between the two methods. The application of 31 livestock effluents on the soil resulted in variability in the experimentally determined CTP for Zn and Cu (0.6 and 1.9 log10 units) comparable to the CTP variability estimated by the reference method for several hundred worldwide soils. These results could be explained by the inadequate prediction of the dissolved organic carbon concentration and Cu and Zn concentration and speciation in soil solution. We propose refinements to the multilinear regressions used for CTP computation that take the soil property patterns following livestock effluent application on soils into account.‡CIRAD; Heavy metal; Manure; Ecotoxicity; Characterisation factors; Fertilisation‡Dataset‡"Dataverse_Clement et al_240709.tab; Input file.tab"‡CC BY-NC-SA 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0‡CIRAD; Heavy metal; Manure; Ecotoxicity; Characterisation factors; Fertilisation‡CIRAD; Heavy metal; Manure; Ecotoxicity; Characterisation factors; Fertilisation‡‡‡‡‡‡

column3: Pascal‡"Ocelet est un langage métier dédié à la modélisation de dynamiques spatiales. Ocelet est doté d'un environnement de développement de modèles et de simulation : Ocelet Modelling Platform. http://www.ocelet.fr/ (2018). Ocelet est notamment basé sur le concept de graphe d'interactions pour manipuler l'information géographique. Ce concept facilite la formalisation de connaissances sur les relations entre entités (spatiales ou non) et sur ce qui provoque l'évolution du système à travers les interactions portées par ces relations. Ce concept permet aussi de représenter des relations spatiales indépendamment de la forme des entités géographiques manipulées (couches thématiques vecteur
column4: ou raster). Le langage Ocelet et la plateforme de modélisation sont développés au sein de l'Unité Mixte de Recherche TETIS (Territoire Environnement Télédétection et Information Spatiale)

Introduction

This archive contains all the necessary data and R script to reproduce the results of the manuscript entitled ""tree growth and mortality of 42 timber species in Central Africa"" submitted to Forest Ecology and Management journal. It includes cleansed data (text files and Rdata files)
column4: computed data (estimates of tree growth and mortality rates
column5: Rdata files)
column6:
R script to reproduce the computation of the estimates as well as the analyses and figures presented in the main paper and an excel files containing all the supplementary material tables of the manuscript.

Cleansed data

To produce the cleansed data
column7: raw data was collected for each sites. The different datasets were standardized to store all of them in a single database. Next
column8:
consecutive diameter measurements were analyzed and some outliers were discarded (see the explanations in the main manuscript). The cleansed data can be loaded using either text delimited csv files or a Rdata file. It contains the five following tables.

Table cleansed_data _species‧csv

This table contains information about each study species. Each line corresponds to one species. It contains the following columns:
  • code : species identifying name
  • timber_name : species name as used by the ATIBT
  • species_name_sci : current scientific species name (genus + species)
  • species_name : vernacular species name
  • dme : reference value of the minimum cutting diameter as defined by the Cameroonian Government
  • incr : reference value of diameter increment (cm/year) as defined by the Cameroonian Government
  • cjb_id : species id of the CJB database
  • see_name : species CJB id of synonym names
  • species_name_sci_full : full current scientific name (genus + species + authority)

Table cleansed_data _observation_codes‧csv

This table contains the description of the codes used in the field to note any particularities of the monitored trees. One line correspond to one code. There are three columns:
  • code : observation code
  • label_fr : French explanation of the code (as used in the field)
  • label_en : English translation of the explanation of the code

Table cleansed_data _mortality_codes‧csv

This table contains the description of the codes used to characterize the likely cause of recorded tree death. There are three columns:
  • code : mortality code
  • label_fr : French explanation of the code (as used in the field)
  • label_en : English translation of the explanation of the code

Table cleansed_data _records‧csv

This table contains the information collected for each tree. Each line corresponds to one record for one tree. There are several lines per tree as they were measured several times. It contains the following columns:
  • site : site name
  • id_site : site identifying number
  • id_plot : plot identifying number
  • treatment : treatment (control
column9:
exploited or mixed).
  • exploitation_year : year of the exploitation (4 digits)
  • species : species vernacular name (corresponding to species_name column of species table)
  • id_tree : tree identifying number
  • number : tree number (the number that was painted on the tree)
  • id : record identifying number
  • date : record date (yyyy-mm-dd)
  • census_year : year of the census
  • diameter : tree diameter measured at hom (cm)
  • diameter450 : tree diameter measured at 450 cm in height (cm)
  • hom : height of measurement of the diameter (cm)
  • code_observation : observation codes. Multiple codes were sometimes used. They are separated by a dash (corresponding to code column of observation_codes table).
  • code_mortality : mortality codes (corresponding to code column of mortality_codes table)
  • comment : any additional comment

    • Table cleansed_data _Increments‧csv

    • id : id of the initial measurement
    • id_tree : tree id number
    • id_plot : plot id number
    • treatment : treatment (control
column10:
exploited)
  • species : species vernacular name (corresponding to species_name of species table)
  • number : tree number (the number that was written on the tree)
  • hom : height of measurement (cm)
  • id_hom : id of HOM (sometimes the HOM had to be changed
column11:
e‧x. due to buttresses or wounds)
  • initial_date : date of the first census
  • initial_diameter : the diameter measured at the first census (cm)
  • diameter_increment : The annual diameter increment computed between the two considered census (cm/year)
  • increment_period : The number of years separating the two censuses (years)

  • diameter_comment : Additional comments written during the two measurements. They are separated by a “/”.

Computed data

From the cleansed data
column15:
we computed - as explained in the main manuscript - tree growth and mortality rates using an R script (3-computation.R). This script produces “computed data”. The computed data contains six tables that are provided with three additional csv files and one RData files.

Computed_data_records‧csv

This table is the same as record‧csv but with one additional column:
  • exploitation_date : the assumed date of the exploitation if any (yyyy-mm-dd)

Table computed_data_growth_rates‧csv

This table contains one line per combination of tree and treatment. It contains the estimates of diameter increment computed over all available records. This table contains the following columns:
  • site : site name
  • id_site : site identifying number
  • treatment : treatment (control or exploited)
  • species : species vernacular name
  • id_plot : plot id number
  • id_tree : tree id number
  • initial_diameter : tree diameter at the begining of the census period (cm)
  • increment_period : length of the census period (year)
  • initial_date : date of the first census (yyyy-mm-dd)
  • diameter_observation : observation codes if any
  • diameter_comment : comment if any
  • exploitation_year : year of the exploitation (4 digits)
  • exploitation_date : assumed date of the last exploitation (if treatement = logged or mixed)
  • mid_point : mid-point of the census period (yyyy-mm-dd)
  • years_after_expl : length of time between the exploitation date and the first measurement
  • n_increment : number of consecutive increment
  • n_hom : number of change of hom during the census period
  • diameter_increment : estimate of the diameter increment (cm/year)

Table computed_data_mortality_rates‧csv

This table contains estimates of mortality rates for each species and site. This table contains the following columns:
  • id_site : site id number
  • treatment : treatment (control or exploited)
  • time_min : minimum of the length of the census periods
  • time_max : maximum of the length of the census periods
  • time_sd : standard deviation of the length of the census periods -- deleted
  • exploitation_year : exploitation year (if treatment = exploited)
  • years_after_expl_mid : number of years between the assumed exploitation and the mid-period census.
  • years_after_expl_start : number of years between the assumed exploitation and the first census.
  • site : site name
  • species : species vernacular name
  • N0 : number of monitored trees
  • N_surviving : number of surviving trees
  • meantime : mean monitoring period length
  • rate : estimates of the mortality rate
  • lowerCI : lower bound of the confidence interval of the mortaltity rate
  • upperCI : lower bound of the confidence interval of the mortaltity rate

File computed_data.Rdata

This Rdata file contains the six tables (species
column17: growth_rates
column18: mortality_rates
column19: mortality_codes
column20:
observation_codes) of the computed data. It can be used to load them in R.

column3: Eric‡In tropical countries
column4: and especially in Sub-Saharan Africa (Sahel and Savannah areas)
column5: subject to significant quantitative and qualitative variations in the availability of fodder during the year
column6:
the dissatisfaction of feed needs is a frequent situation for animals reared in grazing systems without a sufficient supplementation with feed concentrates.
The Body Condition Scoring (BCS) is a useful way to assess the state of body reserves (subcutaneous fat
column7: muscle mass) which reflects the animal's production (growth
column8: milk
column9: work) and reproduction capacities. The evaluation of the body reserves of an animal through the scoring of their body condition is important to adjust their diet and appreciate their general state of health
column10: as well as their reproductive and production capacity (meat
column11: milk
column13: etc.). Managing body reserves is one way of responding to variability in quality and access to feed resources. In females
column14: reserves play an important buffer role during lactation. They can make up for insufficient intakes from the ration. Indeed
column15: the level of production depends on the nutrients provided by the feeds
column16: but also on the animal's body reserves when the feed-based supplies do not fully cover their needs
column17: especially during the dry season. The BCS impacts the interval between two foaling. Overly lean females show a delay in the return of heat after foaling
column18:
the direct consequence of which is the increase in the parturition interval and consequently a decrease in herd productivity.
The assessment of these reserves through Body Condition Scoring (BCS) represents a management tool for livestock farmers
column19: agricultural advisors
column20: and livestock development stakeholders. BCS is a simple
column21: inexpensive and fast method. Several animals can be scored in one session. It allows to compare the BCS of individuals or herds: 1) raised in different production systems or environments
column23: during different seasons (dry season and rainy season). BCS can be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of domestic animal populations. To do this
column24:
BCS alert thresholds and a BCS collection and monitoring system must be defined on reasoned samples of animal populations.
It is an easy-to-use field tool. However
column25: a good mastery of the BCS grid as well as a regular practice of scoring are necessary to obtain precise and reproducible ratings. It can also be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of animal populations. Thus
column26:
the farmer can be called at any time to intervene on the feed ration and / or the health of the animal.
In a study on the well-being of equines in Senegal
column27: Diaw (2013) presents a BCS grid for horses comprising five levels of scoring. To harmonize the Barbe horse BCS grid with other species
column28:
we propose six levels scoring grid accepting the hypothesis that cachectic animals could be present in equine populations which are not considered in the study of Diaw (2013). The proposed grid is partly inspired by the BCS grid for sport horses by Carroll & Huntington (1988) including an BCS scale from 0 to 5.
In 2020
column29: Vall proposed a standardized BCS scoring system for tropical livestock animals for large animals (cattle
column32: and goats) and for donkeys
column33: and horses. This document presents this BCS system applied to the Barbe horses living in Sahel and Savannah areas. We propose a BCS grid with six levels admitting the hypothesis that in the horse populations extreme animals could be present
column34: being very thin (nutritional state worsened by parasitism)
column35: or very fatty.‡CIRAD; body condition scoring; horse; L40 - Anatomie et morphologie des animaux‡‡"BCS_Grid_Barbe_Horse_Senegal_R1.pdf; BCS grid_Barbe horse.xlsx; Dictionary of variables in the Scoring data files.txt; Drawing_Horses_Back view.pdf; Drawings_Horses_Rigth side view.pdf; Pictures_Horses_Back view.pdf; Pictures_Horses_Right side view.pdf"‡CC BY 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0‡CIRAD; body condition scoring; horse; L40 - Anatomie et morphologie des animaux‡CIRAD; body condition scoring; horse; L40 - Anatomie et morphologie des animaux‡‡‡‡‡‡

column3: Lionel‡La cartographie du suivi de la récolte de la canne à sucre a été réalisée en 2021 sur toute la sole cannière de La Réunion. La cartographie présente les dates de début et de fin de récolte d’entités en canne à sucre obtenues par le croisement des couches de l’occupation du sol (Carte d’occupation du sol 2020 Spot-6/7 de niveau3)
column4: du Registre Parcellaire Graphique 2019 et des routes. La chaîne de traitement automatique MASH (Mapping Sugarcane Harvest) cartographie l’avancement de la récolte de canne à sucre en temps semi-réel. La méthode consiste à traiter automatiquement des images satellitaires libres (radar et optique) acquises à haute fréquence (moins de 6 jours). Chaque pixel des parcelles en canne à sucre est classé selon son état de récolte. 32 images satellites en optique (Sentinel-2A et 2B) et 13 images radar (Sentinel-1) du programme Copernicus de l’ESA ont été traitées. column3: Ephraim‡Consumer acceptability of new banana lines and introductions of fresh banana “matooke” cultivars for cooking is important in achieving breeding targets of the IITA and Ugandan breeding programs. column4: 211 spectra have been acquired from 34 genotypes of fresh matooke. The genotypes are part of the IITA collection hosted at Namulonge. Using the developed standard operating procedure developed for fresh matooke
column5: spectra of the flesh of fresh fingers were scanned using an ASD Qualityspec spectrometer. In total 43 replicate measurements for dry matter content were compiled corresponding to the different genotypes (some repeated) were undertaken. Calibrations on the same traits will be considered and the role of NIRS in determination of banana based DM will be undertaken. column3: Jean-Paul‡The database has been created to compile all available data about cottons that have been used in several projects over time with cotton fiber stickiness as a main topic. This includes results from Cirad researches as well as from inter-laboratory trial called round-tests (RT) that were performed over the years with a goal of harmonizing results between instruments as well as between existing characterization methods. These RT were first created for the methods Cirad created (SCT and H2SD) before including RT results organized in partnership with the ITMF-ICCTM committee on stickiness measuring methods. The dataset also aggregates all available results grabbed on some common materials and using various characterization techniques including micro-spinning and industrial spinning. These records concerned both quality and productivity data.	 The main research idea behind this database is to check the relationship of any stickiness measurement result to what spinning experiments data showed in practice in the spinning process that is to be taken as reference

column3: Mélanie‡In tropical countries subject to significant quantitative and qualitative variations in the availability of fodder during the year
column4:
the dissatisfaction of feed needs is a frequent situation for animals raired in grazing systems without a sufficient supplementation with feed concentrates.
The Body Condition Scoring (BCS) is a useful way to assess the state of body reserves (subcutaneous fat
column5: muscle mass) which reflects the animal's production (growth
column6: milk
column7: work) and reproduction capacities. The evaluation of the body reserves of an animal through the scoring of their body condition is important to adjust their diet and appreciate their general state of health
column8: as well as their reproductive and production capacity (meat
column9: milk
column10: work
column11: etc.). Managing body reserves is one way of responding to variability in quality and access to feed resources. In females
column12: reserves play an important buffer role during lactation. They can make up for insufficient intakes from the ration. Indeed
column13: the level of production depends on the nutrients provided by the feeds
column14: but also on the animal's body reserves when the feed-based supplies do not fully cover their needs
column15: especially during the dry season. The BCS impacts the interval between two calving. Overly lean cows show a delay in the return of heat after calving
column16:
the direct consequence of which is the increase in the parturition interval and consequently a decrease in herd productivity.
The assessment of these reserves through Body Condition Scoring (BCS) represents a management tool for livestock farmers
column17: agricultural advisors and livestock development stakeholders. BCS is a simple
column18: inexpensive and fast method. Several animals can be scored in one session. It allows to compare the BCS of individuals or herds: 1) raised in different production systems or environments
column19: 2) or
column20: during different seasons (dry season and rainy season). BCS can be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of domestic animal populations. To do this
column21:
BCS alert thresholds and a BCS collection and monitoring system must be defined on reasoned samples of animal populations.
It is an easy-to-use field tool. However
column22: a good mastery of the BCS grid as well as a regular practice of scoring are necessary to obtain precise and reproducible ratings. It can also be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of animal populations. Thus
column23:
the farmer can be called at any time to intervene on the feed ration and / or the health of the animal.
The BCS grid currently used in the intervention area of the Beef Cattle 2 project
column24:
does not seem to have been developed with reference to the breeds of buffaloes present in Northern Vietnam. This is the reason why we propose this BCS grid adapted to Buffaloes (Bubalus bubalis).

Purpose:

The purpose of this dataset is to provide a high resolution map of available water content for Burkina Faso derived from 30m soil properties maps using the iSDA Africa dataset and the USDA Rosetta3 model. The map can be used to notably support spatialized crop simulation models to determine potential crop yields across West Africa.

Nature and extent of data:

The dataset consists of a high resolution map of plant available water content derived from four soil property datasets from iSDA Africa. The soil properties include sand
column5: clay
column6: silt and fine-earth bulk density
column7: and were predicted at a 30m resolution for 0-20 cm and 20-50cm depth intervals. The USDA Rosetta model was used to compute soil water retention properties from four iSDA layers (sand
column8: clay
column9: silt content
column10: and fine-earth bulk density)
column11: including the van Genuchten parameters of residual water content
column14: 'n' shape parameter
column15: and saturated hydraulic conductivity. From these parameters
column16: the volumetric water content at field capacity and permanent wilting point was computed using the van Genuchten-Mualem model. The output map has been filled with nearest neighbor interpolation where NaN values were present. Finally
column17:
available water content integrated over the soil profile down to 50cm and to the bedrock depth was calculated.

Location and coverage:

The dataset covers the country of Burkina Faso in West Africa at a 30m resolution.

Temporal scope:

The dataset was created using soil property data and models from iSDA Africa and USDA Rosetta3
column18: respectively
column19:
and covers the temporal scope of the original datasets.

column1: https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/MH2XAX‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/MH2XAX‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/MH2XAX‡file://10.18167/DVN1/MH2XAX‡‡doi:10.18167/DVN1/MH2XAX‡2025-03-26T17:46:06Z‡2024-10-15T08:13:32Z‡‡Body Condition Scoring Grid for crossbred Bos taurus × Bos indicus cuban dairy cattle‡Vall
column3: Boris‡In tropical countries subject to significant quantitative and qualitative variations in the availability of fodder during the year
column4:
the dissatisfaction of feed needs is a frequent situation for animals reared in grazing systems without a sufficient supplementation with feed concentrates.
The Body Condition Scoring (BCS) is a useful way to assess the state of body reserves (subcutaneous fat
column5: muscle mass) which reflects the animal's production (growth
column6: milk
column7: work) and reproduction capacities. The evaluation of the body reserves of an animal through the scoring of their body condition is important to adjust their diet and appreciate their general state of health
column8: as well as their reproductive and production capacity (meat
column9: milk
column10: work
column11: etc.). Managing body reserves is one way of responding to variability in quality and access to feed resources. In females
column12: reserves play an important buffer role during lactation. They can make up for insufficient intakes from the ration. Indeed
column13: the level of production depends on the nutrients provided by the feeds
column14: but also on the animal's body reserves when the feed-based supplies do not fully cover their needs
column15: especially during the dry season. The BCS impacts the interval between two calving. Overly lean females show a delay in the return of heat after calving
column16:
the direct consequence of which is the increase in the parturition interval and consequently a decrease in herd productivity.
The assessment of these reserves through Body Condition Scoring (BCS) represents a management tool for livestock farmers
column17: agricultural advisors
column18: and livestock development stakeholders. BCS is a simple
column19: inexpensive and fast method. Several animals can be scored in one session. It allows to compare the BCS of individuals or herds: 1) raised in different production systems or environments
column20: 2) or
column21: during different seasons (dry season and rainy season). BCS can be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of domestic animal populations. To do this
column22:
BCS alert thresholds and a BCS collection and monitoring system must be defined on reasoned samples of animal populations.
It is an easy-to-use field tool. However
column23: a good mastery of the BCS grid as well as a regular practice of scoring are necessary to obtain precise and reproducible ratings. It can also be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of animal populations. Thus
column24:
the farmer can be called at any time to intervene on the feed ration and / or the health of the animal.
In Latin America
column25: few studies have been carried out for developing a BCS grid for cattle and especially devoted to Zébu (Bos Indicus) and Taurines (Bos taurus).  Ayala et al (1995) have developed a BCS grid adapted to Box indicus and crossbred cattle (Box indicus × Holstein and Box indicus × Brown Swiss) in tropical condition (Mexico). However
column26: this BCS still not widely used and the Edmonson et al. (1989) grid
column27:
followed by the Ferguson (1994) grid persists despite its development context being less appropriate to tropical conditions in Latin America.
In 2020
column28: Vall proposed a standardized BCS scoring system for tropical livestock animals for large animals (cattle
column32: and horses. column5: Mathieu‡The overall objective of the ASSET Project is to transform food and agricultural systems in Southeast Asia more sustainable
column6: safer and inclusive
column7: through harnessing the potential of Agroecology.  In this context
column8:
a joint workshop ASSET-ALiSEA was organized by GRET and CIRAD in Laos the 30th of November 2022. An agroecology lexicon composed of 18 concepts and 261 terms was constructed in the context of this workshop. This lexicon could be used for the knowledge hub associated with the ASSET Project.
This dataset contains:
- A presentation that summarizes the content of this workshop and the method used to construct these agroecology lexicons
- The agroecology theoretical lexicon (TL) with terms validated
- The agroecology practical lexicon (PL) co-constructed in Laos
Results:
- Theoretical lexicon (TL): 16 concepts with 331 terms - 138 terms manually validated in the Workshop
- Practical lexicon (PL) constructed in Laos: 18 concepts with 157 terms. 107 terms come from the theoretical lexicon.

column6: Michael‡Gari is a significant

column9: and convenient product from cassava roots in the West Africa sub-region. The granular product is popular in West African countries due to its long shelf life and ease of preparation. It can be consumed by soaking in water or turned into Eba

column17: and stretchability. It is essential to establish the relationship between instrumental and quantitative descriptive sensory scores to present instrumental measurement as a mid-throughput and less costly alternative to the sensory approach
column18: which is subjective. Previous studies have established correlations between the sensory and instrumental measurements for sensory textural attributes of Eba
column19: with a low to medium correlation coefficient. In the current study

column7: Samuel‡Melanaphis sacchari (Zehntner) and Melanaphis sorghi (Theobald) are major worldwide crop pests causing direct feeding damage on sorghum and transmitting viruses to sugarcane. It is common in the scientific literature to consider these two species as synonyms
column8: referred to as ‘Sugarcane Aphid’
column9: although no formal study has validated this synonymy. In this study
column10: based on the comparison of samples collected from the whole distribution area
column11: we use both morphometric and molecular data to better characterize the delineation between M. sacchari and M. sorghi. Unsupervised multivariate analysis of morphometric data clearly confirmed the separation of both species. The most discriminating characters separating both species were based on the length of the antenna processus terminalis relative to the length of hind tibia
column13: none of these criteria alone allow an unambiguous identification. Bayesian clustering based on microsatellite data delimited two clusters
column14: which corresponded to the species separation. The DNA sequencing of three nuclear and three mitochondrial regions revealed a very little divergence between both species. In particular
column15: the COI barcoding region proved to be of limited usefulness for species separation. One SNP located on the nuclear EF1-α gene was diagnostic for species separation. Based on morphological and molecular evidence
column16: the sorghum damaging invasive genotype recently introduced in the USA
column17: Mexico and Caribbean since 2013 is found to be M. sorghi.‡CIRAD; Melanaphis; Aphididae; taxonomy; Ravageurs des plantes‡Dataset‡"morphometry_alates_raw.tab; morphometry_apterous_raw.tab; specimen_metadata.tab; Variables description for raw datafiles.docx; Variables description for specimen metadata.docx"‡CC-BY 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0‡CIRAD; Melanaphis; Aphididae; taxonomy; Ravageurs des plantes‡CIRAD; Melanaphis; Aphididae; taxonomy; Ravageurs des plantes‡English‡-155.8‡150.2‡36.1‡-28.0‡Start Date: 1902-11-10 ; End Date: 2010-03-31

column3: Etienne‡An illustrative model developed with netlogo 5.3.1 for Ecological Complexity paper. This model makes it possible to reproduce the results
column4: to understand their limits and the epistemological scope. Paper abstract : understanding the origins
column5: conditions
column6: advantages
column7: and limitations of cooperation in natural and social systems has motivated many investigations in the biological and social sciences. To investigate individual cooperative behaviour in the face of temporal and spatial heterogeneity of resources we considered a definition of cooperation at the same ontological level as competition. We define a cooperative behaviour when an agent acts upon one or more resource(s) with a beneficial result in at least one resource for a recipient of this action
column8: and with a selection process for this behaviour on the side of the acting agent. We implemented an agent-based model that represents the interactions of agents through their use of resources. With this model
column9: we illustrated how scarcity of resources in space and time might create situations where cooperative behaviour is beneficial to individuals or to egalitarian groups.  Simulations highlighted that temporal scarcity as spatial scarcity of the resource procures advantages to egalitarian groups over competitive individuals. Additionally
column10: the factors favouring equity among agents for the access to the resource promote the success of cooperation. Simulations also showed the limitations imposed by group size on cooperation in the context of a common-pool management system. The possibility of using indicators of (spatio-temporal) resource variability to characterize the potential for the emergence of cooperation is an interesting research objective for future work.‡CIRAD; column3: Samuel Edgar‡"The WP1 Gendered Food Product Profile for boiled potato in Uganda reflects the final step (step 5) of an interdisciplinary five-step methodology developed to identify demand for quality characteristics among diverse user groups along the food chain (Forsythe et al.
column4: 2022). This methodology includes: step 1) interdisciplinary state of knowledge of the product ; step 2) gendered food mapping
column5:
which includes participatory research with men and women in rural communities regarding their product preferences and priorities; step 3) participatory processing diagnosis and quality characteristics ; and step 4) consumer studies in rural and urban areas of the product using different RTB varieties.
Results from step 1 and 2 have been published in Netsayi Noris Mudege

The WP1 Gendered Food Product Profile for potato in Uganda has been agreed by a multidisciplinary team based on the evidence collected on preferred quality characteristics at each step and assessed for their potential harm and benefit for women
column17: based on an adapted G+ tool (publication pending by Forsythe et al.).  The final product profile for boiled potato consisted of most preferred or high quality raw
column18: processing and final product quality characteristics as triangulated from the four steps namely; the state of knowledge
column19: gendered food mapping
column20: participatory processing diagnosis and consumer testing. These were prioritized and analysed for gender impact. The priority high quality raw material characteristics were; red skin colour
column25: firm tuber and Good eyes (few eyes
column27: priority was given to; easy to peel and cooks fast/easy to cook. Final product prioritization consisted of; moderately firm
column30: good boiled potato smell and yellow colour. After gender analysis
column31: red skin colour and ‘no damages’ were deprioritized. Red skin characteristic is mainly associated with one potato variety (Rwangume) and access to information and planting materials are limited which makes the potential benefit hard to realise. Breeding for red skin would be limiting since there are other good characteristics beyond skin colour. Therefore
column32: the women would not realise any significant benefit in breeding for red skin.   On the other hand
column33: damages to potato are mainly due to improper post-harvest handling
column34: which occur in the process of packaging or transportation. It is not a genetic characteristic in potato varieties and breeders may not provide a viable solution for it. Therefore
column35: potato damage presents a considerable harm as it increases drudgery. Mealy was classifies as ‘amend’ or ‘proceed with caution. Reason being that there was need for different levels of mealiness of potato to be available in the market because boiled potato can be processed into other products e‧g. mashed potato products. More breeding is still needed to come up with varying levels of mealiness to target the demand variation. For example
column36: children prefer mashed; when potato is too mealy
column37: it is not good for mashing. Nonetheless
column38: mealy together with good boiled potato smell were classified as ‘nice to have’. Characteristics identified as essential or ‘must have’ from a gender perspective were; moderately big tuber size

column2: Dan‡La construction d’une base de données recensant l’ensemble des espèces de légumineuses présentes dans 3 pays d’Afrique de l’Ouest (Bénin
column3: Burkina Faso et Sénégal) a été initiée dans le cadre de LegAE
column4: un projet de recherche focalisé sur les légumineuses dans un contexte de transition agroécologique et renforcement de la sécurité alimentaire en Afrique. Un état des lieux des connaissances disponibles sur les services écosystémiques (SES) rendus par ces espèces a été mené conjointement. Le travail a été réalisé sur la base d’une revue bibliographique d’envergure et de quelques entretiens réalisés auprès d’experts établis dans la zone d’étude.  La base de données (BDD) recense 148 genres et 558 espèces de légumineuses ; elle rassemble pour chaque espèce les informations recueillies sur sa distribution géographique
column5: ses synonymes et noms d’usage

column7: son caractère cultivé ou non
column8: son type d’habitat(s)
column9: son éventuel référencement sur la liste rouge de l’UICN
column10: enfin sa sensibilité vis-à-vis d’un certain nombre de paramètres biotiques et abiotiques. column4: sorgho

column3: at INRAE Avignon
column4: France‡Ghanem
column5: Otmane‡Ghanem
column6: Otmane‡This document presents the results of physicochemical analyses conducted on eight cassava varieties with contrasting textural properties. Cassava
column7: a staple crop essential for food security in rural Africa
column8: is a major source of carbohydrates. However
column9: improving its post-cooking textural properties is crucial to meet the expectations of both consumers and industries. In this context
column10:
the RTBfoods project aims to develop sustainable and optimized cassava varieties for the food industry.

The analyses focus on extraction yields
column11: including dry matter (DM)
column14: as well as starch composition before and after destarching (residual). Measurements include the levels of neutral sugars
column17: lignin
column18: non-starch polysaccharides (NSP)
column19: and degree of methylation (MD)
column20: along with enzymatic activities of polygalacturonases (PG) and pectin-methylesterases (PME). The results indicate that post-cooking texture is influenced by pectin solubilization
column22:
and interactions between starch and cell wall polysaccharides.

The main objective is to understand the physicochemical mechanisms responsible for textural variations and to develop tools such as MID-IR spectroscopy for rapid

column3: Samuel Edgar‡"The WP1 Gendered Food Product Profile for boiled & steamed sweetpotato in Uganda reflects the final step (step 5) of an interdisciplinary five-step methodology developed to identify demand for quality characteristics among diverse user groups along the food chain (Forsythe et al.
column4: 2022). This methodology includes: step 1) interdisciplinary state of knowledge of the product; step 2) gendered food mapping
column5:
which includes participatory research with men and women in rural communities regarding their product preferences and priorities; step 3) participatory processing diagnosis and quality characteristics ; and step 4) consumer studies in rural and urban areas of the product using different RTB varieties.
Results from step 1 and 2 have been published in Robert O. M. Mwanga

column27:
