The WP1 Gendered Food Product Profile for boiled & steamed sweetpotato in Uganda has been agreed by a multidisciplinary team based on the evidence collected on preferred quality characteristics at each step and assessed for their potential harm and benefit for women
column28:
based on an adapted G+ tool (publication pending by Forsythe et al.).

The Gendered Product Profile for boiled and steamed sweetpotato consisted of most preferred or high quality raw
column29: processing and final product quality characteristics as derived from the state of knowledge
column31: participatory processing diagnosis and consumer testing. These were prioritized after triangulation and then subjected to gender analysis. At raw material the priority characteristics were; smooth skin
column32: produces sap when broken
column33: large root
column34: hard root and sweet taste. Processing characteristics were; cooks fast (easy to cook) and reduced oxidation while the final product priority was; mealy
column37: firm and not fibrous. After gender analysis
column38: ‘produces sap when broken’ was deprioritized (rejected). The reasons were that sweetpotato requires moderate sap and breeding for more amount of sap would increase drudgery it would become difficult to wash and more water would be used by women. If sweetpotato is bred for more sap
column39: it would only be beneficial for planting where it increases weevil resistance but would be rejected by consumers because it would require more resources for processing into boiled product. A change in the current amount of sap in sweetpotato would therefore bring in a considerable harm to the food chain. More so
column40: G+ analysis showed that smooth skin was classified as ‘amend’ or ‘proceed with caution’ as it would make the roots more marketable and there would be a high potential for men to displace women in marketing. Therefore there would be  need to integrate a program towards empowering women with marketing skills
column41: and training in good post-harvest handling practices. Women should also be provided with access to more seeds
column42: extension workers and other packages developed by agronomists among other mitigation measures. All the other aforementioned characteristics were evaluated as being essential ‘must have’."‡CIRAD; sweet potatoes; gender analysis; high quality characteristics; product profile; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡Dataset‡"RTBfoods_G+ Food Product Profile_Boiled & steamed sweetpotato_Uganda.xlsx"‡CC BY-NC-SA 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0‡CIRAD; sweet potatoes; gender analysis; high quality characteristics; product profile; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡CIRAD; sweet potatoes; gender analysis; high quality characteristics; product profile; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡Not applicable‡‡‡‡‡

column4: Céline‡Le domaine pastoral des Hautes-Pyrénées s’étend sur plus de 140 000 ha et est utilisé de façon collective par près de 1 200 éleveurs de début juin à fin octobre. La gestion de ces territoires
column5: appartenant quasi exclusivement aux collectivités locales
column6: est assurée par différentes entités : communes
column7: commissions syndicales

column9: Groupements pastoraux
column10: Associations Foncières Pastorales. Ces gestionnaires d’estives (GE) organisent l’accueil des troupeaux
column11: assurent la majorité des travaux pastoraux et les opérations de gestion. Pour ce faire
column12: ils disposent de diverses ressources : taxes de pâturage (baccades)
column14: subventions et contrats agri-environnementaux.  La contractualisation agri-environnementale dans le cadre du 2eme pilier de la Politique Agricole Commune (PAC) date de plus de trente ans et prend la forme entre 2015 et 2023 de mesures agro-environnementale et climatiques (MAEC). Ces mesures souscrites volontairement par des exploitations agricoles ou des entités collectives (i.e. les GE dans notre cas) pour une durée de 5 ans
column15: ont pour objectif de soutenir des changements de pratiques en faveur de la protection de l’environnement (MAEC localisées: Ouvert03 ou Herbe09 ) ou le maintien de pratiques (MAEC système herbagers et pastoraux - SHP).  Cette enquête s’inscrit dans le projet de recherche-action Contracts2.0 (Co-design of novel contract models for innovative agri-environmental-climate measures and valorisation of environmental public goods) financé par la Commission Européenne.  Le CIRAD a travaillé avec le Groupement d’intérêt public CRPGE (Centre de Ressources sur le Pastoralisme et la Gestion de l’Espace) des Hautes Pyrénées pour étudier l’effet des mesures agro-environnementales sur la gestion collective des estives
column16: et la mise en place avec les acteurs de terrain de dispositifs de concertation pour envisager l’évolution de ces contrats. column1: https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/BP2GO8‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/BP2GO8‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/BP2GO8‡file://10.18167/DVN1/BP2GO8‡‡doi:10.18167/DVN1/BP2GO8‡2025-03-26T17:39:07Z‡2024-10-15T08:30:47Z‡‡Body Condition Scoring Grid for Sheep (Sahel and Savannah areas)‡Vall
column3: Eric‡In tropical countries subject to significant quantitative and qualitative variations in the availability of fodder during the year
column4:
the dissatisfaction of feed needs is a frequent situation for animals reared in grazing systems without a sufficient supplementation with feed concentrates.
The Body Condition Scoring (BCS) is a useful way to assess the state of body reserves (subcutaneous fat
column5: muscle mass) which reflects the animal's production (growth
column6: milk
column7: work) and reproduction capacities. The evaluation of the body reserves of an animal through the scoring of their body condition is important to adjust their diet and appreciate their general state of health
column8: as well as their reproductive and production capacity (meat
column9: milk
column10: work
column11: etc.). Managing body reserves is one way of responding to variability in quality and access to feed resources. In females
column12: reserves play an important buffer role during lactation. They can make up for insufficient intakes from the ration. Indeed
column13: the level of production depends on the nutrients provided by the feeds
column14: but also on the animal's body reserves when the feed-based supplies do not fully cover their needs
column15: especially during the dry season. The BCS impacts the interval between two lambing. Overly lean females show a delay in the return of heat after lambing
column16:
the direct consequence of which is the increase in the parturition interval and consequently a decrease in herd productivity.
The assessment of these reserves through Body Condition Scoring (BCS) represents a management tool for livestock farmers
column17: agricultural advisors and livestock development stakeholders. BCS is a simple
column18: inexpensive and fast method. Several animals can be scored in one session. It allows to compare the BCS of individuals or herds: 1) raised in different production systems or environments
column19: 2) or
column20: during different seasons (dry season and rainy season). BCS can be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of domestic animal populations. To do this
column21:
BCS alert thresholds and a BCS collection and monitoring system must be defined on reasoned samples of animal populations.
It is an easy-to-use field tool. However
column22: a good mastery of the BCS grid as well as a regular practice of scoring are necessary to obtain precise and reproducible ratings. It can also be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of animal populations. Thus
column23:
the farmer can be called at any time to intervene on the feed ration and / or the health of the animal.
In African regions
column24: most of the sheep breeds belongs to slender type
column25: with elongated legs and poorly developed muscular masses. Fat is mainly deposited on internal organs (e.g. intermuscular). According to Richard (1997)
column26: it is possible to identify a scale of variation according to the general individual body condition following criteria similar to those used in Europe. Inspired in the body condition scoring grid (from 0 to 5) developed by Russel et al. (1969)
column27:
Richard (1997) proposed a scoring system for Fulani sheep widely present in the Sahelian regions.
In 2020
column28: Vall proposed a standardized BCS scoring system for tropical livestock animals for large animals (cattle
column32: and horses. This document presents this BCS system applied to the Sahelian goat. For harmonizing the BCS method to be used in sheep with regard to that used for other species

column3: Céline‡"Dataset of the paper entitled ""Does a decade of soil organic fertilization promote copper and zinc bioavailability to epi-endogeic earthworms?"" While long-term organic fertilizer (OF) application tends to decrease copper (Cu) and zinc (Zn) availability in agricultural soils
column4: earthworm bioturbation tends to have the opposite effect. Consequences in terms of Cu and Zn bioavailability to earthworms in bioturbated
column5: OF-amended soils are hence still a matter of debate. Accordingly
column6: we assessed the effect of a decade of agronomically-realistic OF applications on Cu and Zn availability in earthworm-inhabited soils and their consequence on Cu and Zn bioavailability to earthworms. An epi-endogeic species (Dichogaster saliens) was exposed in microcosms to three soils receiving at field scale either no
column7: mineral or organic fertilization for a decade. Dissolved organic matter (DOM) properties (i‧e. concentration
column8: aromaticity
column9: and binding properties towards Cu)
column10: pH
column11: and Cu and Zn availability (i‧e. total concentration and free ionic activity) were determined in the solution of earthworm-inhabited soils. Cu and Zn bioavailability was assessed by measuring the net accumulation and concentration of Cu and Zn in earthworms. Despite soil Cu and Zn contamination induced by a decade of OF applications
column12: the organic fertilization-induced increase in pH and DOM properties drove the mitigation of Cu and Zn availability in earthworm-inhabited soils
column13: while bioturbation had little effect. Consistently
column14: Cu and Zn bioavailability did not increase with OF applications. From an ecotoxicological perspective
column15: our results suggest that agronomically-realistic applications of OF for a decade should not constitute a risk for earthworms in terms of bioaccumulation and toxicity of Cu and Zn."‡CIRAD; Soil amendments; Soil Analysis; Pollution; Fertilizing; Soil chemistry and physics‡Dataset‡"Dataverse_earthw-Cu-Zn-bioavailability_220124.tab"‡‡‡CIRAD; Soil amendments; Soil Analysis; Pollution; Fertilizing; Soil chemistry and physics‡CIRAD; Soil amendments; Soil Analysis; Pollution; Fertilizing; Soil chemistry and physics‡‡‡‡‡‡

column3:
Ernest Ogbete‡Cassava (Manihot esculenta Crantz) is a major root crop in the tropics and its starchy roots are significant sources of calories for more than 500 million people worldwide especially Nigeria. Cassava breeders aim to produce many clones rapidly with desired agronomic traits.
Near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) is a physical detection method that was developed in recent decades. This technique is based on the correlation between chemical properties and the absorption of light at specific wavelengths in the near-infrared region.
A rapid and accurate method for determining dry matter (DM) content of cassava root will be valuable and help the breeders in selecting clones with good traits.

This database contains the spectra of fresh intact cassava roots harvested at NRCRI Umudike Nigeria (between August and September 2020) with the use of Portable/handheld NIRS (Quality SpecTrek). A total number of 1764 spectra were acquired from 98 (ninety-eight) clones of fresh intact cassava roots. Three different sizes of cassava roots (big
column4: medium and small sized) were cut in horizontal positions C1 (proximal)
column5: C3 (central)
column6: and C5 (distal). Calibration model developed for dry matter content (DM) showed the mean and standard deviation to be 39.44 and 4.73 respectively with the standard error of laboratory at 0.76. An SOP on the protocol for the determination of DM on fresh intact cassava root using a handheld NIRS has been linked to this database. The ASD hand-held NIRS was acquired by NRCRI (National Root Crops Research Institute) Umudike Nigeria in the year 2016.‡CIRAD; cassava; dry matter; standard deviation; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡Dataset‡"RTBfoods_K.2.29_Spectral database_NIRS_Dry Matter_Intact Fresh Cassava_NRCRI Nigeria_2020.xlsx"‡‡‡CIRAD; cassava; dry matter; standard deviation; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡CIRAD; cassava; dry matter; standard deviation; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡English‡‡‡‡‡

column1: https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/DFIN8U‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/DFIN8U‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/DFIN8U‡file://10.18167/DVN1/DFIN8U‡English‡doi:10.18167/DVN1/DFIN8U‡2025-02-06T10:18:39Z‡2025-01-29T16:45:30Z‡‡Apple trees in agroforestry – Investigating the plasticity of vegetative and reproductive traits. Mediterranean area of France during 2018 to 2023‡Lauri
column3: Pierre-Éric‡Agroforestry is promoted as a way to improve the sustainability of horticultural systems through plant diversification and also to mitigate climate change through carbon sequestration. It could also alleviate excessive light and temperature in high-radiation regions of the world. However
column4: little is known about the long-term shade adaptation of the temperate fruit tree in agroforestry systems. A study was developed to investigate apple growing under walnut trees in two shade conditions compared to a full light condition. Our aim was to quantify the plasticity of traits and the covariations between traits in these three light conditions using a multiscale approach considering different scales from whole tree to annual shoot and inflorescence. Shade did not affect the height of the apple trees
column5: while it reduced the diameter of the trunk and branches. On the other hand
column6: the total number of growing shoots was reduced in shade
column7: and flowering and fruiting were fewer and more irregular than in full light. Strikingly
column8: at the whole tree scale
column9: covariations between vegetative traits (trunk cross-sectional area versus mean branch cross-sectional area) and between vegetative and reproductive traits (trunk cross-sectional area versus total number of inflorescences) were not altered by shade. However
column10: at the shoot scale
column11: return-bloom was significantly reduced by shade
column12: whereas at the inflorescence scale shade did not affect leaf number or leaf area. We propose a shade adaptation syndrome that includes not only shade intensity but also shade dynamics during the growing season and over consecutive years
column13: in mediterranean area of France during 2018 to 2023.‡CIRAD; tree architecture; tree functioning; trait covariation; flowering; fruit-set; shade adaptation syndrome; shade trees; shade tolerance; Juglans regia; Malus; F62 - Physiologie végétale - Croissance et développement‡Dataset‡"Dataset_Lauri_Gautier_2024_data_Fig1.tab; Dataset_Lauri_Gautier_2024_data_Fig2_a_b.tab; Dataset_Lauri_Gautier_2024_data_Fig2_c.tab; Dataset_Lauri_Gautier_2024_data_Fig2_d.tab; Dataset_Lauri_Gautier_2024_data_Fig4_a_b.tab; Dataset_Lauri_Gautier_2024_data_Fig4_c_d_e_f.tab; Dataset_Lauri_Gautier_2024_data_Fig6.tab; Dataset_Lauri_Gautier_2024_data_fig7.tab; Dataset_Lauri_Gautier_2024.tab"‡CC BY 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0‡CIRAD; tree architecture; tree functioning; trait covariation; flowering; fruit-set; shade adaptation syndrome; shade trees; shade tolerance; Juglans regia; Malus; F62 - Physiologie végétale - Croissance et développement‡CIRAD; tree architecture; tree functioning; trait covariation; flowering; fruit-set; shade adaptation syndrome; shade trees; shade tolerance; Juglans regia; Malus; F62 - Physiologie végétale - Croissance et développement‡English‡‡‡‡‡Start Date: 2018-01-01 ; End Date: 2024-03-31

column3: François‡The overall objective of the ASSET Project is to transform food and agricultural systems in Southeast Asia more sustainable
column4: safer and inclusive
column5: through harnessing the potential of Agroecology.  In this context
column6:
a joint workshop ASSET-ALiSEA was organized by GRET and CIRAD in Vietnam the 30th of November 2022. An agroecology lexicon composed of 19 concepts and 175 terms was constructed in the context of this workshop. This lexicon could be used for the knowledge hub associated with the ASSET Project.
This dataset contains:
- A presentation that summarizes the results of this workshop
- The agroecology theoretical lexicon (TL) with terms validated
- The agroecology practical lexicon (PL) co-constructed in Vietnam
Results:
- Theoretical lexicon (TL): 16 concepts with 331 terms - 76 terms manually validated in the Workshop
- Practical lexicon (PL) constructed in Cambodia: 16 concepts (+ 3 concepts associated with the farming systems) with 175 terms. 68 terms come from the theoretical lexicon.

Workshop outputs.‡Goutard
column3: Flavie‡"Technical
column4: regulatory
column5: or awareness strategies can be implemented to reduce the usage of antibiotics in livestock production to fight against antimicrobial resistance. However
column6: they can be ineffective due to the misalignment with farmers’ realities. This study aimed to co-develop with relevant stakeholders’ strategies to reduce the usage of antibiotics in chicken production in Vietnam
column7: a country with high use of antibiotics
column8: using the ImpresS ex-ante approach. Three workshops were organized a week apart with chicken farmers
column9: drug sellers’ representatives
column10: public and private veterinarians
column11: a chicken retailer
column12: and academic staff operating at the communal
column14: or provincial level. Participants have co-developed the vision that they would like to reach in 10 years to reduce the usage of antibiotics
column16: changes that must be done to overcome these problems and finally they have designed two strategies to address these changes.	 This dataset includes the raw results and the observers' reports for the three workshops.  - Common vision:  Result of Workshop 1. ""Yellow card"" on which participants wrote on a yellow sheet of paper the vision to be achieved in ten years to reduce the use of antibiotics in chicken production in the province
column17: that was then grouped by themes and compiled to develop the common vision. - Problem Tree: Result of Workshop 1. The participants build problem trees by writing problems on a red card that prevents the realization of the common vision. - Report 1: Result Workshop 1. Report of the observers. - Outcome Pathway: Result Workshop 2. Identification by the participants of barriers to achieve the common vision (red card)
column18: changes that need to be made (orange card) and stakeholders that need to achieve the changes (green card)
column19: obstacles to the change (red cards) and strategies to be implemented (blue card). - Report 2: Result Workshop 2. Report of the observers. - Report 3: Result Workshop 3. Thomas‡"

Crop evolutionary history and domestication processes are key issues for better conservation and effective use of crop genetic diversity. In Western Africa
column5: two small indigenous grain cereals are found
column6:
black and white fonio (Digitaria exilis and Digitaria iburua respectively). The relationship between these two cultivated crops and wild Digitaria species is still unclear.

We analysed whole genome sequences of 265 accessions comprising the two cultivated species and their closely wild relatives.

We used different complementary methods and genomic approaches to unravel the domestication history of these fonio millets
column7:
which is key to support a process of valorisation of these native species while preserving the diversity necessary for future adaptations.

We provide here the passport data
column8: the vcf files
column9:
and the kmer files used to perform the analyses. Codes can be accessed in the CIRAD GitLab repository

Two compressed VCF files are provided. They contain high quality biallelic SNPs. One is filtered for Minor Allele Frequency (MAF) at 5%. Both vcf.gz are associated to their corresponding index file .tbi

Different extensions for the kmer analysis files are provided and correspond to the 1
column11:
000 k-mer presence/absence table for each individual.

column2: Sarah‡Alexandre Chloé‡The DeSIRA (Development Smart Innovation through Research in Agriculture) Initiative from the European Commission funds Research and Development projects seeking to bridge the gap between the research community and the formulation of policies to build resilient
column3: sustainable and equitable agri-food systems in the Global South. These projects are accompanied by DeSIRA-LIFT (Leveraging the DeSIRA Initiative for the Transformation of Agri-Food Systems)
column4:
which provides services to prove and improve their impact.

In the context of the DeSIRA-LIFT initiative
column5: we aim to mobilize text-mining methods to characterise the nature of the innovations developed by the DeSIRA projects via the documents produced at different stages of the projects. To enable characterising innovation from textual document
column6: we built a lexicon dealing with (1) the actors involved in these processes ; (2) triggering factors and issues to be resolved ;  (3) the nature of the process through which actors are engaged ; (4) its components or outputs (new technology
column7: new process
column8: new service
column9: new organization
column10:
new policy etc.) ; (5) the themes or domains tackled; and (6) the innovation maturity level.

Lexicon - 1st version (desira-lift-v1.ods)
Three researchers on agricultural innovation (CA
column11: AT
column13: with the support of the “Keops Team”
column14: the lexicon's overall architecture. Keywords were then  identified by combining 3 types of resources :  <ol>  <li> The researchers proposed keywords based on previous scientific work on agricultural innovation

We built upon the first version of the Desira-LIFT lexicon and proposed an enhanced version. Three major changes have been made: (1) new keywords were added after a new phase of consultation with the experts

column3: Lionel‡La cartographie du suivi de la récolte de la canne à sucre a été réalisée en 2017 sur toute la sole cannière de La Réunion. Les images satellites radar et optique du programme Copernicus de l'ESA sont disponibles à haute fréquence de revisite pour ce suivi. La cartographie présente les dates de début et de fin de récolte d’entités cannières obtenues par le croisement des couches du registre parcellaire graphique (RPG)
column4: du cadastre et des routes. La chaîne de traitement MASH (Mapping Sugarcane Harvest) a été développée pour automatiser les traitements afin de cartographier l’avancement de la récolte de canne à sucre en temps semi-réel. La méthode consiste à traiter automatiquement des images satellitaires libres (radar et optique) acquises à haute fréquence (moins de 6 jours). Chaque pixel des parcelles en canne à sucre est classé selon son état de récolte.‡CIRAD; télédétection; système d'information géographique; calendrier cultural; Sentinel-1; Sentinel-2; F01 - Culture des plantes‡Dataset‡"Lisez-moi_2017_recolte_canne_Reunion.txt"‡CC BY-SA 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0‡CIRAD; télédétection; système d'information géographique; calendrier cultural; Sentinel-1; Sentinel-2; F01 - Culture des plantes‡CIRAD; télédétection; système d'information géographique; calendrier cultural; Sentinel-1; Sentinel-2; F01 - Culture des plantes‡French‡-21.386113967473253‡-20.88928529208309‡‡‡Start Date: 2017-07-01 ; End Date: 2017-12-31

column3:
Frédéric‡"This is the list of all the functional annotations about the 797 nsLTP sequences used for our work ""Comprehensive classification of the plant non-specific lipid transfer protein superfamily towards its Sequence – Structure – Function analysis"".
It might be useful for any studies in sequence-structure-function relationships (this is the largest dataset used in a phylogeny study to date).
The information is provided as a csv and an xlsx file organized as follows
Column A contains the database ID used for our study.
Column B contains the nsLTP Type (if available and as stated by initial authors) together with the organism 5 letters code.
Column C: LTP name
Column D: UniProt page if it exists (URL)
Column E: protein sequence (without propeptide)
The annotations are displayed in column G to L with ontology (e‧g.: GO for Gene Ontology) then the term of the ontology and a link to its description
column4: the level of evidence (see guide : http://geneontology.org/docs/guide-go-evidence-codes/)
column5: additional information
column6:
reference in the literature (author + article DOI)
If a second annotation is available
column7:
the same kind of information is added in new columns on the right.

Stéphane‡"The land use maps published here were produced for the municipalities studied by the DINAAMICC project during the period 2016-2017 (based on data previously collected by other projects). They cover the entire Itasy region and part of the Analamanga and Vakinankaratra regions.
This period was chosen for the availability of the field surveys needed to train and validate the algorithms for producing land cover maps. The data used are cited in the ""Related datasets"" category below. The choice of a simplified nomenclature divided into two hierarchical levels made it possible to create a database of 4
column7:
245 polygons.
The images used are the Sentinel-2 level 2A images distributed by THEIA acquired between September 2016 and June 2017.
Considering the distribution of the municipalities studied by the project
column8: the available field surveys and the absence of very high spatial resolution images over the area during this period
column9:
it was decided to use the Iota² chain on 4 Sentinel-2 tiles.
The overall accuracy of the maps obtained is 87% for level 1 and 78% for level 2.

column3: Emmanuel‡Copper (Cu) is potentially toxic trace elements present in large amounts in organic wastes (OWs) spread on agricultural lands as fertilizer. Cu speciation in OW is a crucial parameter to understand their fate in soil after spreading and to assess the risk associated with agricultural recycling of OW. To explore the spatial heterogeneity of Cu species at the µm scale from raw OWs up to digestates and composts
column4: we used a laterally resolved synchrotron techniques
column5:
the so-called fluorescence-XANES imaging approach.

For each organic wastes
column6: an initial large area survey scan at 15.8 keV has been conducted to identify the area of interest and obtain overall elemental distributions.  Subsequently
column7:
a smaller area has been chosen to conduct fluorescence XANES imaging with the XANES stack itself consisting of individual maps at decreasing energies across the Cu K-edge.

These analyses have been conducted for 7 organic wastes:
Urban-3_Raw_OW
Urban-3_Compost
Agri-1_Raw_OW
Agri-1_Digestate
Agri-1_Compost
Central-1_Raw_OW

column3: the Government of Uzbekistan established the Lower Amu Darya State Biosphere Reserve (LABR). This reserve aims to conserve the Tugay
column4: an endangered riparian forest ecosystem straddling the main rivers of Central Asia’s drylands
column5: which is under extreme anthropogenic pressure. The LABR has reintroduced Bukhara red deer (Cervus hanglu bactrianus)
column6: a subspecies endemic to Asia whose numbers declined severely over the 20th century.  The LABR development project aims to provide operational support to the Uzbek authorities for their application to join the World Network of Biosphere Reserves. GIZ (German Society for International Cooperation) requested CIRAD to provide a science-based estimate of the deer population in the LABR
column7: using an internationally recognized method
column8: and to issue recommendations to ensure ecologically and socio-economically sustainable management.  The survey of the Bukhara red deer population was carried out in October 2019. column3: Estelle‡Macroinvertebrates play a central role in processes supporting soil fertility. In the framework of the ecological intensification of agriculture
column4: the choice of management practices should be guided by their ability to support these beneficial organisms supplying ecosystem services. This study aims at investigating the specific effect of partial substitution of synthetic fertilizers by locally produced organic fertilizers at a similar level of major nutrient inputs on macroinvertebrate’s abundance and diversity in sugarcane agroecosystems
column5: on a Nitisol. Invertebrates visible to the naked eye were sampled in 2013
column7: 2019 and 2023 using the standardized TSBF method on a long term experimental field trial in Réunion island. The individuals were identified and soil samples were analyzed for physico-chemical properties. Despite the low response of macroinvertebrates to the fertilizer type
column8: total macroinvertebrate abundance increased over time
column9: especially isopods and earthworms. The input of organic carbon via the return of litter to the soil surface and the root turnover after each harvest enables soil macroinvertebrates to be more abundant even after the replanting tillage. Mulching and root turnover are therefore important levers to consider for promoting macroinvertebrates in sugarcane agroecosystems.‡CIRAD; soil amendments; soil fauna; soil quality; soil chemicophysical properties; mulches; Fertilization; Fertilizing; Soil chemistry and physics‡Dataset; Taxonomy; Biological data; Experimental data‡"Dataverse_Macrofauna_240229.xlsx; Field trial design_Macrofauna.pdf"‡CC BY-NC-SA 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0‡CIRAD; soil amendments; soil fauna; soil quality; soil chemicophysical properties; mulches; Fertilization; Fertilizing; Soil chemistry and physics‡CIRAD; soil amendments; soil fauna; soil quality; soil chemicophysical properties; mulches; Fertilization; Fertilizing; Soil chemistry and physics‡‡‡‡‡‡Start Date: 2013-01-01 ; End Date: 2023-12-31

column2: Lionel‡La cartographie du suivi de la récolte de la canne à sucre a été réalisée en 2019 sur toute la sole cannière de La Réunion. La cartographie présente les dates de début et de fin de récolte d’entités cannières obtenues par le croisement des couches de l’occupation du sol (Carte d’occupation du sol 2018 Spot-6/7  de niveau3)
column3: du Registre Parcellaire Graphique 2017 et des routes. La chaîne de traitement automatique MASH (Mapping Sugarcane Harvest) cartographie l’avancement de la récolte de canne à sucre en temps semi-réel. La méthode consiste à traiter automatiquement des images satellitaires libres (radar et optique) acquises à haute fréquence (moins de 6 jours). Chaque pixel des parcelles en canne à sucre est classé selon son état de récolte. 33 images satellites en optique (Sentinel-2A et 2B) et 14 images radar (Sentinel-1) du programme Copernicus de l’ESA ont été traitées. Le fichier vectoriel est accessible à tous les agents du CIRAD sur la plateforme du Cirad AWARE pour la diffusion des données géographiques.‡CIRAD; télédétection; système d'information géographique; calendrier cultural; Sentinel-1; Sentinel-2; F01 - Culture des plantes‡Dataset‡"Lisez-moi_2019_recolte_canne_Reunion.txt"‡CC BY-SA 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0‡CIRAD; télédétection; système d'information géographique; calendrier cultural; Sentinel-1; Sentinel-2; F01 - Culture des plantes‡CIRAD; télédétection; système d'information géographique; calendrier cultural; Sentinel-1; Sentinel-2; F01 - Culture des plantes‡French‡-21.386113967473253‡-20.88928529208309‡‡‡Start Date: 2019-07-01 ; End Date: 2019-12-15

P. Langbour and J. Gérard. 2018. New formula and conversion factor to compute basic wood density of tree species using a global wood technology database. American Journal of Botany. 105(10): 1-9. [DOI: 10.1002/ajb2.1175]. bioRxiv: 274068

Cirad (https://www.cirad.fr) is the French agricultural research and international cooperation organization working for the sustainable development of tropical and Mediterranean regions. Since the 1950s
column9:
scientists at Cirad have compiled data on wood characteristics for hundreds of tree species around the world. Data have been compiled in a unique global wood technology database.

Combining our research results with the data from the wood technology database
column10: we were able to compute the basic wood density for each tree and derive the Cirad wood density database (CWDD). The CWDD is available as a comma-separated values file: Cirad-wood-density-database‧csv. It includes basic wood-density data Db (in g/cm3) for 4022 trees collected in 63 countries (mostly tropical) and representing 872 species. Basic wood-density has been computed from the wood-density at 12% moisture D12 (in g/cm3)
column11: the volumetric shrinkage coefficient R (in %/%)
column12: and the fibre saturation point S (in %) for 3832 trees. When R or S values were not available (for 190 trees)
column13:
the basic wood-density was estimated from D12 using a conversion factor of 0.828: Db = 0.828 D12.

column3: Eric‡In tropical countries
column4: and especially in Sub-Saharan Africa (Sahel and Savannah areas)
column5: subject to significant quantitative and qualitative variations in the availability of fodder during the year
column6:
the dissatisfaction of feed needs is a frequent situation for animals reared in grazing systems without a sufficient supplementation with feed concentrates.
The Body Condition Scoring (BCS) is a useful way to assess the state of body reserves (subcutaneous fat
column7: muscle mass) which reflects the animal's production (growth
column8: milk
column9: work) and reproduction capacities. The evaluation of the body reserves of an animal through the scoring of their body condition is important to adjust their diet and appreciate their general state of health
column10: as well as their reproductive and production capacity (meat
column11: milk
column13: etc.). Managing body reserves is one way of responding to variability in quality and access to feed resources. In females
column14: reserves play an important buffer role during lactation. They can make up for insufficient intakes from the ration. Indeed
column15: the level of production depends on the nutrients provided by the feeds
column16: but also on the animal's body reserves when the feed-based supplies do not fully cover their needs
column17: especially during the dry season. The BCS impacts the interval between two calving. Overly lean females show a delay in the return of heat after calving
column18:
the direct consequence of which is the increase in the parturition interval and consequently a decrease in herd productivity.
The assessment of these reserves through Body Condition Scoring (BCS) represents a management tool for livestock farmers
column19: agricultural advisors
column20: and livestock development stakeholders. BCS is a simple
column21: inexpensive and fast method. Several animals can be scored in one session. It allows to compare the BCS of individuals or herds: 1) raised in different production systems or environments
column23: during different seasons (dry season and rainy season). BCS can be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of domestic animal populations. To do this
column24:
BCS alert thresholds and a BCS collection and monitoring system must be defined on reasoned samples of animal populations.
It is an easy-to-use field tool. However
column25: a good mastery of the BCS grid as well as a regular practice of scoring are necessary to obtain precise and reproducible ratings. It can also be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of animal populations. Thus
column26:
the farmer can be called at any time to intervene on the feed ration and / or the health of the animal.
In Sub-Saharan Africa
column27: many studies have been carried out for developing a BCS grid for cattle and especially devoted to Zébu (Bos Indicus): Pullan (1978) for the White Fulani zebu of Nigeria; Nicholson & Butterworth (1989) for zebu (Bos indicus) in general; Cisse (1995) for the Gobra zebu; Vall et al. (2002) for White and Red Fulani draft zebus in Northern Cameroon; Vall & Bayala (2004) for Sudanese zebus). For the BCS grid presented into this document
column28: we relied mainly on systems based on six level of BCS that is the most frequent (Pullan
column33: Vall proposed a standardized BCS scoring system for tropical livestock animals for large animals (cattle
column37: and horses. column3: Kephas‡"The WP1 Gendered Food Product Profile for Matooke in Uganda reflects the final step (step 5) of an interdisciplinary five-step methodology developed to identify demand for quality characteristics among diverse user groups along the food chain (Forsythe et al.
column4: 2022). This methodology includes: step 1) interdisciplinary state of knowledge of the product ; step 2) gendered food mapping
column5:
which includes participatory research with men and women in rural communities regarding their product preferences and priorities; step 3) participatory processing diagnosis and quality characteristics ; and step 4) consumer testing studies in rural and urban areas of the product using contrasted RTB varieties.
Results from step 1 and 2 have been published in Kenneth Akankwasa

column8: Moreen Asasira
column9: Elizabeth Khakasa
column10: Innocent Mpirirwe
column11: Uli Kleih
column14: Dominique Dufour & Kephas Nowakunda (2021). The East African highland cooking bananas ‘Matooke’ preferences of farmers and traders: Implications for variety development. International Journal of Food science and Technology
column16:
1124-1134. https://doi.org/10.1111/ijfs.14813.

The WP1 Gendered Food Product Profile for Matooke in Uganda has been agreed by a multidisciplinary team based on the evidence collected on preferred quality characteristics at each step and assessed for their potential harm and benefit for women
column17:
based on an adapted G+ tool (publication pending by Forsythe et al.).

‘Matooke’ is a staple food made from highland cooking bananas in the Great Lakes region of East Africa. Improvement of bananas for resistance to pests and diseases has been a major breeding objective of the national breeding programme. This product profile for matooke presents the quality characteristics among different user groups in Uganda basing on a participatory and interdisciplinary method. The Matooke Food Product Profile is essentially a description of a high-quality food product from an evolving list of sensory
column18: processing and agronomic characteristics that reflects different preferences among users from a gender perspective. This involved aggregating evidence on preferences with farmers
column19: traders
column20: processors and consumers
column21: where important characteristics and their indicators were identified and prioritised
column23:
agreed by a multidisciplinary team together with a gendered livelihoods assessment.
Key raw material characteristics identified include: big fingers for they ease the preparation process (easy to peel
column24: high peel yield and less time used)
column25: big bunch (high market value
column26: food for home use). Creamy pulp colour
column27: shiny green finger colour and disease free are other important characteristics. The gender analysis of the final list of characteristics
column28: which used an  adapted G+ tool
column29: resulted in big bunch being de-prioritised. Although this characteristic was noted as essentially important
column30: it can place a labour burden especially on women in cases of harvesting
column31: carrying to their homes for preparation for example
column32: if women carry for long distances from farms for home consumption/paying to carry on bicycle
column33:
need for technology promotional adjustments (need to prioritise an attainable and acceptable threshold.
At processing
column38: straight fingers and relatively low sap were preferred. Low sap saves both time and money in terms of washing the pulp
column39: soap to clean the sticky sappy hands and utensils
column40: and too much sap would alter the colour of both pulp and mashed food. However
column41:
some sap is important and most varieties in the market have varying sap content. Therefore prioritising breeding more for this this trait might result in undesired amount of sap undesired by users.
At the end product stage
column45: good matooke taste and holding together when mashed were the most important end product characteristics. Through the gender assessment
column46: it was learned that a yellow colour might be attained at first boiling/steaming
column47: it may require additional time and fuel (simmering) for some varieties
column48: which may seem costly in terms of time and money."‡CIRAD; matooke; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡Dataset‡"RTBfoods_G+ Food Product Profile_Matooke_Uganda.xlsx"‡CC BY-NC-SA 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0‡CIRAD; matooke; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡CIRAD; matooke; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡Not applicable‡‡‡‡‡

column3:
Stéphane‡The map proposed here is a key data to understand urban agriculture’s dynamics in relation to urbanization in Antananarivo agglomeration and agriculture’s contribution to the city food system while debates are ongoing to update Antananarivo city master plan.
This land cover map was obtained using the Moringa treatment chain
column4: a prototype chain developed by CIRAD (TETIS Joint Research Unit). It is based on the combined use of a very high spatial resolution Pleiades image acquired on 8 January 2017 by the ISIS system of the CNES (French Space Agency) and high spatial image time series Sentinel-2 and Landsat-8 for a period from October 2016 to September 2017
column5:
in a classification approach combining OBIA (object based image analysis) and data mining (Random Forest) trained by a reference database collected in situ. The processing is done with free software (Orfeo Toolbox and Python).
The field database consists of field surveys conducted with GPS device. The surveys were conducted in 2017 during the raining season. Outline of the plots were drawn on the Pleiades image. This database of 3068 plots was then divided in two: 80% of the plots per class were randomly selected to form the learning database and the remaining 20% was selected to form the validation database.
This map was developed within the frame of a research project (“Legend project”) supported by the metaprogramme GloFoodS (Transitions to global food security) jointly conducted by INRA and Cirad
column6:
the two leading French research institutions for agriculture (www.glofoods.inra.fr).
The objective of our study was to test an innovative mapping method in an area of great complexity where urban and agriculture are intertwined : the Antananarivo’s agglomeration.‡CIRAD; télédétection; remote sensing; agriculture; environnement; environment; base de données spatiale; spatial database; Landsat-8; Sentinel-2; landsat; Pleiades; Imagerie/Cartes de base/Occupation des terres; Imagery / Base Maps / Earth Cover‡Dataset‡"Tana_LCM_2017_level1.tar.gz; Tana_LCM_2017_level2.tar.gz; Tana_LCM_2017_level3.tar.gz; Tana_LCM_2017_level4.tar.gz"‡CC BY 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0‡CIRAD; télédétection; remote sensing; agriculture; environnement; environment; base de données spatiale; spatial database; Landsat-8; Sentinel-2; landsat; Pleiades; Imagerie/Cartes de base/Occupation des terres; Imagery / Base Maps / Earth Cover‡CIRAD; télédétection; remote sensing; agriculture; environnement; environment; base de données spatiale; spatial database; Landsat-8; Sentinel-2; landsat; Pleiades; Imagerie/Cartes de base/Occupation des terres; Imagery / Base Maps / Earth Cover‡English; French‡47.2727755622‡47.716218709‡‡‡Start Date: 2016-10-01 ; End Date: 2017-09-30

column3: Morgane‡We present the first videos of consumption of bat or rodent by rusty-spotted genet (Genetta maculata) inside a cave in the Republic of Congo. Following the implementation of a camera-trap monitoring protocol of interactions between cave bats and wildlife
column4: we identified important genet activity in one of the caves in our study. Between 2022 and 2023
column5: we recorded four events of bats or rodents’ consumption (including two with certainty on bats)
column6: one hunting behavior on bats and three feeding behaviors on insects. We detail the various behaviors and then discuss the potential implications of genets consuming bats
column7: rodents and insects
column8: and in particular scavenging on dead bats. Finally
column9:
we address the potential implications of zoonotic pathogen transmission from bats transmitted by genets to humans via the bushmeat trade.

column3: Céline‡We assessed the effect of a decade of agronomically realistic organic fertilization on copper (Cu) and zinc (Zn) availability in the rhizosphere and their phytoavailability. Using a laboratory biotest
column4: Festuca arundinacea was exposed to 34 soil samples collected from three agricultural field trials that had received no
column5: mineral
column6: or organic fertilization for a decade. Dissolved organic matter (DOM) properties (i‧e.
column7: concentration
column8: aromaticity
column9: and binding properties toward Cu)
column10: pH
column11: and Cu and Zn availability (i‧e.
column12: total dissolved concentration and free ionic activity) were determined in the rhizosphere solutions. Cu and Zn phytoavailability was measured as the plant uptake flux. Contrary to bulk soils
column13: organic fertilization induced very few changes in the chemistry and Cu and Zn availability in the rhizosphere solutions compared to no and mineral fertilization. Consistently
column14: Cu and Zn phytoavailability did not increase with organic fertilization
column15: but it was mostly driven by soil properties rather than by fertilization. Despite increasing soil Cu and Zn contamination
column16: a decade of soil organic fertilization did not increase Cu and Zn phytoavailability

column2: Gordon‡Pectin methylesterase (PME) activity correlates well with texture in potatoes (Solanum tuberosum); higher PME activity is associated with increased firmness as the PME rapidly demethylates pectin in the cell walls and middle lamellae
column3: allowing for hardening through cross linking with divalent cations. In order to understand cooked sweet potato root texture
column4: an assay was developed for PME activity PME removes methyl groups from pectin producing methanol and free acids. A simple and sensitive method for determination of methanol is important in the study of pectin methyl esterase (PME) in plant material. A determination of the amount of methanol produced by a particular plant tissue can be used to assess the level of PME activity or
column5: indeed
column6: determine the degree of methylation of purified pectins. An approach to assay methanol is to oxidize the methanol to formaldehyde and then colorimetrically determine the formaldehyde. The oxidation is carried out by alcohol oxidase. This enzymatic method improves selectivity and also eliminates the use of hazardous chemicals. The formaldehyde produced is condensed with N-methylbenzothiazolinone-2-hydrazone (MBTH) under neutral conditions. When the medium is acidified and an oxidant (i‧e.
column7: Fe3+) is added
column8: this adduct then forms a blue formazan dye that can be determined colorimetrically. Assess PME activity in roots of sweet potato genotypes known to have different textural properties. column2: Lionel‡L’UMR TETIS du CIRAD développe une méthode de cartographie d’occupation du sol automatisée fondée sur la chaîne Moringa dans le cadre du Centre d’Expertise Scientifique Occupation des Sols de THEIA. La méthodologie consiste en une segmentation d’une image à Très Haute Résolution Spatiale (identification d'objets homogènes sur l'image THRS) puis une classification de chaque objet homogène par l’extraction d’indices d’une ou de plusieurs séries temporelles d’images à haute résolution spatiale ; la classification est entrainée sur une base de données d’apprentissage réalisée par photo-interprétation. La collaboration entre l’UMR Tétis et l’UR AïDA a permis la production de  la carte d’occupation du sol pour l'année 2021. Elle utilise une mosaïque d'images Spot6 pour la segmentation puis 63 images Sentinel2 pour la classification. La base de données de terrain est constituée de plus de 5000 objets actualisés en 2021 et caractérisés selon 3 niveaux d’une nomenclature emboîtée comportant de 4 à 29 classes. Le niveau le plus détaillé distinguant les types de cultures présente une précision globale de 89% et un indice de Kappa est de 0
column3: 88. Le niveau 2 (12 classes)
column4: distinguant les groupes de cultures présente une précision globale de 92% et un indice de Kappa est de 0
column5: 91. Le niveau 1 (4 classes)
column6: distinguant les grands groupes d'occupation du sol présente une précision globale de 98% et un indice de Kappa est de 0
column7: 96. La carte d'occupation du sol

column3:
Ernest Ogbete‡Cassava breeders aim at producing many clones rapidly with desired nutritional and agronomic traits. Near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) is a physical detection method that was developed in recent decades. This technique is based on the correlation between chemical properties and the absorption of light at specific wavelengths in the near-infrared region. A rapid and accurate method for determining starch yield of cassava root will be valuable and help the breeders in selecting clones with good traits from a large population in breeding programmes.

This data base contain the spectra of fresh intact cassava genotypes harvested at NRCRI
column4: Umudike
column5: Nigeria (between August and September 2020) with the use of Portable/handheld NIRS (Quality SpecTrek). A total number of 3168 spectra were acquired from 176 (one hundred and seventy six) clones of fresh intact cassava roots for determining starch yield of the fresh cassava roots. Three different sizes of cassava roots (big
column6: medium and small sized) were cut in horizontal positions C1(proximal)
column7: C3(central)
column8: C5(distal). Calibration model developed for the starch yield showed the mean and standard deviation to be 20.39 and 3.80 respectively with the standard error of laboratory at 0.89. An SOP on the protocol for the determination of starch yield on fresh intact cassava root using a handheld NIRS has been linked to this database. The ASD hand-held NIRS was acquired by NRCRI (National Root Crops Research Institute) Umudike Nigeria in the year 2016.‡CIRAD; cassava; starch yield; starch; standard deviation; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡Dataset‡"RTBfoods_K.2.30_Spectral database_NIRS_Starch_Intact Fresh Cassava_NRCRI Nigeria_2020.xlsx"‡‡‡CIRAD; cassava; starch yield; starch; standard deviation; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡CIRAD; cassava; starch yield; starch; standard deviation; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; food analysis; Food science and Technology‡English‡‡‡‡‡

column1: https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/OQ13EV‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/OQ13EV‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/OQ13EV‡file://10.18167/DVN1/OQ13EV‡English‡doi:10.18167/DVN1/OQ13EV‡2024-04-09T14:58:08Z‡2023-01-31T15:37:36Z‡‡NIRS calibration database on fresh mashed Matooke for color & texture at NaCRRI & IITA Uganda‡RTBfoods Project Management Unit‡Uwimana
column2:
Brigitte‡This database contains 1312 NIR spectra of fresh mashed banana samples acquired in NaCRRI (Uganda) by using FOSS DS2500 NIR spectrometer.

Banana bunches are harvested at the green-mature stage: when the finger angles are filled but before the fingers start ripening. Each bunch is labeled by genotype and bunch number. Cluster 2 of each bunch is used for acquiring the NIRS on the harvesting day
column3: according to the protocol described in the SOP. Four random fingers of cluster 2 are selected and divided into two equal halves
column4: giving a total of eight halves. Four of the 8 halves are first used for texture analysis using a TA.XT (Stable Micro Systems) texture analizer. The same samples are then mashed using a mottle and a pestle and used for NIRS acquisition. The remaining samples are used to estimate dry matter content
column5: using an oven on 50 g of fresh matter. Cluster 3 of the same bunch is used for colour analysis using a chromameter (CR-400). This dataset combines NIRS spectra

column3:
Didier‡[FR] Données sur les analyses chimiques des échantillons de sols et feuilles des cacaoyers (Theobroma cacao L.) prélevés dans des plantations de cacaoyers adultes sélectionnées dans toutes les régions du sud de la Côte d'Ivoire.
.................................
Les échantillons de sols et feuilles des cacaoyers (Theobroma cacao L.) ont été prélevés dans des plantations de cacaoyers adultes sélectionnées dans toutes les unités pédologiques de toutes les régions du sud de la Côte d'Ivoire. Les échantillons de sol ont été prélevés dans l'horizon de 0 à 30 cm à 1 m des cacaoyers. Les échantillons de sol ont été analysés pour connaître leur teneur en argile et leurs paramètres chimiques; à savoir pH
column4: C
column5: N
column6: P
column7: K
column8: Ca
column9: Mg
column10: CEC
column11: Al
column14: Mo
column15:
Mn.
Les échantillons de feuilles ont été prélevées en mars 2015. Les récoltes de l'année 2015 ont été estimées à partir du comptage des cabosses.
Chaque parcelle de cacaoyer échantillonnée a été géolocalisée pour être associée à l'unité pédologique du sol correspondante. L'information a ensuite été utilisée pour construire une carte thématique fournissant des recommandations d'engrais localisées pour la culture du cacao (voir publication N'Guessan et al.
column16:
2017).

column3: Ephraim‡The objective of this database was the determination of the influence of Dry Matter (DM) and rapid visco analyser (RVA) parameters on the acceptability of matooke and could explain relevant traits such as tokeness in banana. The fresh matooke  for tokeness database was created out of the RTB clones established at NACCRI (Uganda). column3: Mélanie‡In tropical countries subject to significant quantitative and qualitative variations in the availability of fodder during the year
column4:
the dissatisfaction of feed needs is a frequent situation for animals reared in grazing systems without a sufficient supplementation with feed concentrates.
The Body Condition Scoring (BCS) is a useful way to assess the state of body reserves (subcutaneous fat
column5: muscle mass) which reflects the animal's production (growth
column6: milk
column7: work) and reproduction capacities. The evaluation of the body reserves of an animal through the scoring of their body condition is important to adjust their diet and appreciate their general state of health
column8: as well as their reproductive and production capacity (meat
column9: milk
column10: work
column11: etc.). Managing body reserves is one way of responding to variability in quality and access to feed resources. In females
column12: reserves play an important buffer role during lactation. They can make up for insufficient intakes from the ration. Indeed
column13: the level of production depends on the nutrients provided by the feeds
column14: but also on the animal's body reserves when the feed-based supplies do not fully cover their needs
column15: especially during the dry season. The BCS impacts the interval between two calving. Overly lean cows show a delay in the return of heat after calving
column16:
the direct consequence of which is the increase in the parturition interval and consequently a decrease in herd productivity.
The assessment of these reserves through Body Condition Scoring (BCS) represents a management tool for livestock farmers
column17: agricultural advisors
column18: and livestock development stakeholders. BCS is a simple
column19: inexpensive and fast method. Several animals can be scored in one session. It allows to compare the BCS of individuals or herds: 1) raised in different production systems or environments
column20: 2) or
column21: during different seasons (dry season and rainy season). BCS can be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of domestic animal populations. To do this
column22:
BCS alert thresholds and a BCS collection and monitoring system must be defined on reasoned samples of animal populations.
It is an easy-to-use field tool. However
column23: a good mastery of the BCS grid as well as a regular practice of scoring are necessary to obtain precise and reproducible ratings. It can also be used as a tool for monitoring and alerting the nutritional level of animal populations. Thus
column24:
the farmer can be called at any time to intervene on the feed ration and / or the health of the animal.
The BCS grid currently used in the intervention area of the Beef Cattle 2 project
column25:
does not seem to have been developed with reference to the breeds of cattle present in Northern Vietnam. This is the reason why we propose this BCS grid adapted to Yellow Cattle (crossbred Bos indicus x Bos taurus).
In 2020
column26: Vall proposed a standardized BCS scoring system for tropical livestock animals for large animals (cattle
column27: camels)
column30: and horses. This document presents this BCS system applied to the Yellow cattle.‡CIRAD; body condition scoring; cattle; L40 - Anatomie et morphologie des animaux‡‡"BCS Grid_Ndama cattle.xlsx; BCS_Grid_Yellow_Cattle_Vietnam_R1.pdf; BCS Scoring data_Ndama cattle.xlsx; Dictionary of variables in the Scoring data files.txt; Drawings_Ndama cattle_Back view.pdf; Drawings_Ndama cattle_Rigth side view.pdf; Pictures_Ndama cattle_Back view.pdf; Pictures_Ndama cattle_Rigth side view.pdf"‡CC BY 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0‡CIRAD; body condition scoring; cattle; L40 - Anatomie et morphologie des animaux‡CIRAD; body condition scoring; cattle; L40 - Anatomie et morphologie des animaux‡‡‡‡‡‡

Stéphane‡As part of THEIA (the French Data and Services center for continental surfaces) CIRAD's TETIS research unit is developing an automated mapping method based on the Moringa chain that minimizes interactions with users by automating most image analysis and processing.
The methodology uses jointly a Very High Spatial Resolution image (Spot6/7 or Pleiades) and one or more time series of High Spatial Resolution optical images such as Sentinel-2
column5:
Landsat-8 and Sentinel-1 for a classification combining segmentation and object classification (use of the Random Forest algorithm) driven by a learning database constituted from in situ collection and photo-interpretation.
The land use maps
column6: in ESRI shapefile format
column7:
are produced as part of the GABIR project (Gestion Agricole des Biomasses à l'échelle de l'Ile de la Réunion) and are all distributed on CIRAD's spatial data catalogue in Réunion: http://aware.cirad.fr/
This Dataverse entry concerns the maps produced
column8: for the year 2019
column9: using a mosaic of Spot6/7 images to calculate segmentation (extraction of homogeneous objects from the image). We use a field database with a nested nomenclature with 3 levels of accuracy allowing us to produce a classification by level. column2: Lionel‡L’UMR TETIS du CIRAD développe une méthode de cartographie d’occupation du sol automatisée fondée sur la chaîne Moringa dans le cadre du Centre d’Expertise Scientifique Occupation des Sols de THEIA. La méthodologie consiste en une segmentation d’une image à Très Haute Résolution Spatiale (identification d'objets homogènes sur l'image THRS) puis une classification de chaque objet homogène par l’extraction d’indices d’une ou de plusieurs séries temporelles d’images à haute résolution spatiale ; la classification est entrainée sur une base de données d’apprentissage réalisée par photo-interprétation. La collaboration entre l’UMR Tétis et l’UR AïDA a permis la production de  la carte d’occupation du sol pour l'année 2020. Elle utilise une mosaïque d'images Spot6 pour la segmentation puis 61 images Sentinel2 pour la classification. La base de données de terrain est constituée de près de 5000 objets actualisés en 2020 et caractérisés selon 3 niveaux d’une nomenclature emboîtée comportant de 4 à 29 classes. Le niveau le plus détaillé distinguant les types de cultures présente une précision globale de 88% et un indice de Kappa est de 0
column3: 87. Le niveau 2 (12 classes)
column4: distinguant les groupes de cultures présente une précision globale de 91% et un indice de Kappa est de 0
column5: 90. Le niveau 1 (4 classes)
column6: distinguant les grands groupes d'occupation du sol présente une précision globale de 98% et un indice de Kappa est de 0
column7: 96. La carte d'occupation du sol
column8: produite au format vecteur Esri Shape

column3: Lionel‡La cartographie du suivi de la récolte de la canne à sucre a été réalisée en 2018 sur toute la sole cannière de La Réunion. 30 images satellites en optique (Sentinel-2A et 2B) et 12 images radar (Sentinel-1) du programme Copernicus de l’ESA ont été traitées. La cartographie présente les dates de début et de fin de récolte d’entités cannières obtenues par le croisement des couches de l’occupation du sol (Carte d’occupation du sol 2017 Spot-6/7 (niveau3) disponible sur Aware)
column4: du cadastre et des routes. La chaîne de traitement MASH (Mapping Sugarcane Harvest) a été développée pour automatiser les traitements afin de cartographier l’avancement de la récolte de canne à sucre en temps semi-réel. La méthode consiste à traiter automatiquement des images satellitaires libres (radar et optique) acquises à haute fréquence (moins de 6 jours). Chaque pixel des parcelles en canne à sucre est classé selon son état de récolte. column3: Aude‡"This dataset is accompanying the manuscript ""Integrating ethnolinguistic and archaeobotanical data to uncover the origin and dispersal of cultivated sorghum in Africa: a genomic perspective""
column4:
available on bioRxiv under the accession 2025.04.16.648676 and is under consideration for recommendation in Peer Community in Evolutionary Biology.

The aim of the study was to shed light on the domestication process of sorghum (its origin and diffusion) by integrating ethnolinguistic
column5: archaeobotanical and genomic data. The origin and spread of cultivated sorghum were considered to result from both biological and social processes
column6: with two leading questions: firstly
column7: how human social groups could have contributed to the dissemination of initial sorghum populations
column8: and secondly
column9:
how linguistic barriers could have subsequently contributed to the isolation of sorghum populations from each other and to the building of its current genetic structure.

We analysed genotyping-by-sequencing data of 210 african accessions of cultivated sorghum using population genetics approach and spatially-explecit modelling to analyse the population diversity and sorghum of African cultivated sirghum
column10: and to infer its area of origin
column11:
as well as the timing of the onset of its expansion and the speed rate of its initial diffusion.

The dataverse repository consist in the genotypic data (vcf format; data_sorgho_210CoreColl‧vcf)
column13: and the table with the information on the archaeological sites considered in the study (ArcheoSitesSorghum‧txt)
column14:
.

column3: Ephraim‡Cassava root softness is one of the important consumer acceptability traits for boiled root cassava product. Thus determination of fresh root softness using relevant phenotyping tools makes it possible to select cassava varieties with preferred farmer attributes. The fresh grated cassava for softness database was created out of the RTB clones established at NaCRRI. The samples constituting this database were harvested in 2019 where 58 genotypes were considered. The database is powered by the standard operating procedure on acquiring spectra from fresh grated roots using the FOSS DS2500 equipment. Given logistical and technical constraints
column4: the database consists of 36 of the 58 genotypes RTB panel genotypes. Spectra from these genotypes were obtained from fresh grated immediately after harvest. At the same time
column5: reference information related to the softness of the root was generated using a penetrometer and texture analyzer. Calibration on the same showed weaker association of the trait (r<0.4) with spectra acquired. This shows that relevant adjustments in methodologies to acquire spectra and number of clones used are needed to fully determine the potential of NIRS to determine root softness in cassava.‡CIRAD; cassava; root softness; firmness; phenotyping; spectroscopy; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; calibration; food analysis; spectral data; Food science and Technology‡Dataset‡"RTBfoods_K.2.25_Spectral database_NIRS_Softness_Fresh Grated Cassava_NaCRRI Uganda_2020.tab"‡CC BY-NC-SA 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0‡CIRAD; cassava; root softness; firmness; phenotyping; spectroscopy; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; calibration; food analysis; spectral data; Food science and Technology‡CIRAD; cassava; root softness; firmness; phenotyping; spectroscopy; NIRS; infrared spectrophotometry; calibration; food analysis; spectral data; Food science and Technology‡English‡‡‡‡‡

column3: Denis‡Cornet
column4: Denis‡The quality of pounded yam is a key criterion for the adoption of new varieties by both producers and consumers. Its assessment appears to be multifactorial. To date
column5: no single compositional trait—such as amylose content
column6: pectins
column7: or dry matter—can reliably predict this quality. These traits are
column8: in many cases
column9:

column3: Cyrane‡"These data support the research article <b>""Repellency of wild oregano plant volatiles
column4: Plectranthus amboinicus

column7: on tomato ”. We tested three origins of wild oregano
column8: including northern (WO1)
column9:
central (WO2) and southern (WO3) Martinique (French West Indies). 🍅

Olfactometric bioassays were carried out with a vertical still-air olfactometer and we evaluated the behavioral response of B. tabaci to wild oregano extracts. The olfactometer was exposed to white light and 10 to 15 whiteflies were introduced in the bottom of olfactometer. We tested ethanolic solution of WO essential oil applied on filter paper disc or tomato leaflet disc placed at the top of olfactometer. We also tested essential oil of winter savory (WS).Three modalities were tested: 50
column10:
100 and 200ng of essentials EO per disc at the same time as two control olfactometers. One of the control is a empty olfactometer called air and the other control olfactometer had filter paper or tomato leaflet.
We also tested ethanolic solution of volatile organic compounds. VOCs of individual wild oregano plant were collected during 24h then were eluted with 200 µL of pure ethanol. 15 ethanolic VOC solution per origin were tested on filter paper disc or tomato leaflet disc using the method described above.

An experiment was conducted in an insect-proof cage in a greenhouse and we compared the distribution of B. tabaci adults and egg laying between between different associations after release whiteflies. Four modalities were studied: tomato alone (tomato)
column11: tomato with WO1 (T+WO1)
column12: tomato with WO2(T+WO2) and tomato with WO3. A test consisted of four growing trays
column14:
and it was repeated six times.

The document provides data collected in Martinique (2019). For olfactometric bioassays
column15: we counted the number of whiteflies in the top of olfactometer and the other part. Then we calculated the proportion of whiteflies in the top of olfactometer. For the greenhouse test
column16: we counted the number of B. tabaci adults at three times
column17: 3 h
column18:
24 h and 96 h after release. We also counted the number of eggs 96h after release. The data are the tables in excel.

Essential oils and ethanolic solution of VOCs were analyzed with a gas chromatograph (Varian 450 GC) equipped with a DB5 MS non-polar capillary column (30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm

Contribution du giroflier à la sécurité alimentaire des ménages agricoles dans la région de Fénérive-Est
column3: Eric‡Fourcin
column4: Claire‡L’étude présentée ici fait partie du projet commandité par l’Union Africaine ASF4Food qui vise à renforcer la sécurité alimentaire et le bien-être des ménages ruraux africains via l’association entre cultures vivrières et systèmes agroforestiers. Cette étude s’est déroulée à Madagascar
column5: dans le district de Fénérive-Est et a pour objectif de comprendre l’importance relative des différentes cultures et en particulier des girofliers
column6: de l’élevage et des activités extra-agricoles dans la constitution des revenus et la robustesse des exploitations agricoles
column7: ainsi que de comprendre comment la sécurité alimentaire est assurée par les systèmes d’activités
column8: au sein de deux sites. L’analyse se base principalement sur une démarche quantitative de performances technico-économiques à travers deux échelles : les cultures et les systèmes d’activités. La caractérisation des structures d’exploitation agricole a mis en évidence les deux principales stratégies élaborées par les exploitants pour subvenir aux besoins alimentaires de leur ménage. La seconde s’appuie sur les revenus du ménage pour acheter la nourriture nécessaire
column9: le giroflier fait alors figure de première source de revenu pour plus de la moitié des ménages enquêtés. Les girofliers fournissent deux produits
column10: les clous et l’huile essentielle. La production d’huile essentielle est une variable d’ajustement stable dont la production à une grande influence sur les volumes et les fluctuations de la production de clous. La fluctuation de la production de clous impose aux ménages une gestion pluriannuelle du solde de trésorerie
column11: l’élevage bovin à un rôle de capitalisation qui permet cette gestion particulière du solde de trésorerie. La gestion des girofliers entre production de clous et d’huile essentielle admet une marge d’amélioration. Enfin
column12: les modélisations prospectives ont montré la grande robustesse des exploitations agricoles due à la flexibilité de leurs dépenses
column13: mais parfois fragilisées par le trop grand rôle des girofliers.‡CIRAD; clou de girofle; systèmes agroforestiers; Fénérive-Est; Madagascar; revenu; modélisation; exploitation agricole; modélisation revenu exploitation agricole .‡Dataset‡"Madagascar fenerive est EAs enquêtées 2014 anonym.tab; Vavatenina_2016 anonym.OLY"‡CC-BY 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0‡CIRAD; clou de girofle; systèmes agroforestiers; Fénérive-Est; Madagascar; revenu; modélisation; exploitation agricole; modélisation revenu exploitation agricole .‡CIRAD; clou de girofle; systèmes agroforestiers; Fénérive-Est; Madagascar; revenu; modélisation; exploitation agricole; modélisation revenu exploitation agricole .‡‡‡‡‡‡

column3: Stéphane‡Land use map
column4: in ESRI shapefile format
column5:
displaying the evolution of artificial spaces in Antananarivo agglomeration (Madagascar capital) during 2003 to 2017 period.
This map is the result of remote sensing processing of satellite images. Spot5 images acquired on February 14 and July 4
column6:
2003 were used. Access to the images was granted by CNES via the ISIS program. The treatment consisted in using the land use map extracted on the same study zone in 2017 (Cf. reference below) as a mask and checking on the old images the evolution of the built spaces.

We tried to measure agricultural areas evolution during the same period but the robustness of the results is not satisfactory because of the difficulty in distinguishing between agricultural and natural areas on 2003 images.

Result validation was carried out using a reference database created by photo-interpretation by an independent operator. The overall accuracy of the map is 91.63% and kappa index is 0.88.

Description: This dataset provides daily interpolated rainfall maps for Martinique island
column6: for the period 1980-2021
column7: at 0.001° resolution
column8:
derived from daily rain gauge data observations.

Dataset Preparation Methods: These maps were obtained by performing interpolation from daily rain gauge data (Martinique ORSTOM-DIREN dataset v1 doi:10.5281/zenodo.10263910)
column9:
using ordinary kriging with fitting of Exponential variogram models. Resolution of interpolation is 0.001°. The daily results were assembled into yearly netCDF files.

column10: Vincent‡A retrospective case control study conducted in France between 2022 and 2023
column11: involving 20 farms (10 case and 10 control farms)
column12: investigated the factors influencing the sanitary quality of raw milk cheeses
column13: focusing on the agricultural aspect and the bacterial microbiota characterised using 16S rRNA short reads metabarcoding from cheese production and the environment during manufacturing. The microbiological quality of raw milk cheeses is defined in Europe by Regulation (EC) No. 2073/2005. In this study
column14: a set of food safety criteria related to the presence of food-borne pathogens such as Salmonella
column15: Listeria monocytogenes and virulence factors of STEC in cheese for a long-time period (2017- 2022) prior to sampling was used to select the case farms. Using multivariate analysis
column16: this study identified multiple bacterial species and critical sampling points such as raw milk
column17: milking machines and wooden shelves that contribute to food-borne contamination of cheeses and highlights the importance of farm management practices such as additional vaccination or transhumance
column18: hygiene and manufacturing practices to ensure the safety of raw milk cheeses. The bacterial species from raw milk cheeses or contact materials along the production chain contributing to the case or control farms were diversified in winter and summer. This study confirms the value of multi-block analysis for managing complex datasets and suggests that 16S rRNA markers from metabarcoding can help to identify critical control points and risk factors.‡CIRAD; column3: Thaïlande‡Penot
column4: Eric‡Stroesser
column5: laetitia‡L’étude présentée ici a été réalisée dans le cadre du projet Heveadapt
column6: qui cherche à analyser comment les plantations familiales peuvent s’adapter et demeurer viables face à des conditions climatiques variables et à de profonds changements socio-économiques. Elle s’est déroulée en Thaïlande
column7: dans la province de Phatthalung
column8: et s’est intéressée aux systèmes agroforestiers dont la composante principale est l’hévéa. L’objectif est de comprendre l’importance relative dans la constitution des revenus et dans la robustesse des exploitations
column9: de l’hévéa
column10: des autres cultures et de l’élevage associés dans la même parcelle
column11: ou au sein de l’exploitation
column12:
et des activités extra-agricoles.
L’analyse s’appuie principalement sur la quantification de performances socio-économiques à deux échelles : les systèmes de culture et les systèmes d’activité. La caractérisation des structures d’exploitation agricole a mis en évidence les deux principales stratégies des planteurs pour maintenir leurs revenus face à la volatilité des prix du caoutchouc naturel. La première consiste à associer d’autres espèces à l’hévéa
column13: au sein du même système de culture
column14: ou dans des systèmes différents. Le système agroforestier qui semble être le meilleur compromis entre la valorisation de la terre et celle du travail familial associe un mélange d’espèces fruitières et forestières aux hévéas. Dans la seconde stratégie
column15:
les planteurs développent des activités extra-agricoles pour compléter le revenu du ménage.
Ces deux stratégies peuvent être combinées à divers degrés. Enfin
column16: les modélisations prospectives ont montré la résilience d’une partie des exploitations agricoles
column17: face à la volatilité à la baisse des prix du caoutchouc naturel
column18: due à la flexibilité de leurs systèmes agroforestiers. Celles qui ne le sont pas sont fragilisées par le rôle encore trop important des hévéas.‡CIRAD; Thailand; Thaïlande; modelisation; modelling; Olympe; revenu agricole; revenu de l'exploitation; farm income; modélisation des revenus agricoles.‡Dataset‡"article etudes rurales du développement-Sorbonne strosser PENOT et al thailande_last .pdf; data EA Heveadapt_PM strosser 2015 anonym.oly; data exploitation 2015 stroesse EA_FG anonym2.xls; données stroesser 2015 FT_incomes analysis anonym 2 .xlsx; données stroesser 2015 FT_incomes analysis non anonym .xlsx; Rapport_Stroesser thailande 2015.pdf"‡CC BY-NC 4.0‡http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0‡CIRAD; Thailand; Thaïlande; modelisation; modelling; Olympe; revenu agricole; revenu de l'exploitation; farm income; modélisation des revenus agricoles.‡CIRAD; Thailand; Thaïlande; modelisation; modelling; Olympe; revenu agricole; revenu de l'exploitation; farm income; modélisation des revenus agricoles.‡French‡‡‡‡‡

column1: https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/F3ZLPS‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/F3ZLPS‡https://doi.org/10.18167/DVN1/F3ZLPS‡file‡‡doi:10.18167/DVN1/F3ZLPS‡2024-04-23T12:57:18Z‡2020-10-01T12:55:18Z‡‡Rôle et impact du girofle sur la formation du revenu des exploitations agricoles de  Sainte Marie
column2: Madagascar  Modélisation par le logiciel olympe d’une exploitation par type et par village 2013‡Penot
column3: Eric‡Richard
column4:
Annabelle‡Les principaux objectifs de cette étude sont les suivants ; comprendre et répondre aux questions suivantes :
Quelle est la contribution de la filière girofle à la formation des revenus des exploitations de l’île Sainte Marie ?
Quelle est la contribution du girofle dans la résilience des exploitations ?
Nous allons nous intéresser à l’impact du girofle et de cette diversification sur le fonctionnement et la formation des revenus des exploitations agricoles locales. Notre hypothèse principale est que le girofle contribue significativement à la résilience globale des exploitations alors que la ressource est vieillissante pas ou peu renouvelée dans un contexte d’aléas climatiques importants de type cyclonique.
Les exploitations représentatives de la typologie faite en 2012 par S. Levasseur et al. seront modélisées et on appréciera la place des deux filières du girofle (clous et essence) au sein des exploitations de Sainte Marie.
